set.analysis_note: Adam's fear arises from exposed nakedness before God, driving him to hide—the inner state is shame-triggered dread of divine presence, felt immediately in the will as flight.
Terror is an instilled inner disposition placed upon all animals toward humanity after the flood, making every creature fear humans as a divinely ordered reality.
Fear of humanity is decreed upon all living creatures; this fear functions as an embedded inner orientation in animals that ensures human dominion over the natural world.
Fear directed toward humans is placed upon every creature as a permanent condition; it shapes animals' inner response to mankind, producing avoidance and submission.
God commands Abram not to fear, grounding the call on God's own protective presence as shield—the fear addressed is an inward anxiety about threat, countered by divine reassurance.
Terror descends on Abram alongside deep sleep and great darkness—the inner impact is an overwhelming, externally imposed dread that accompanies a visionary encounter with God.
Sarah's fear is an inward reaction to being caught in a lie before a divine visitor; it drives her to deny what she said, revealing fear as a self-protective impulse.
Lot's fear of Zoar governs his will, compelling him to abandon the city and live in a cave—the inner state directly determines his choice of dwelling and way of life.
Abimelech's servants are struck with intense fear upon hearing what God had revealed—the inner state is a corporate, acute dread produced by learning of divine intervention in their lives.
Abraham assumes the absence of God-fear in Gerar's people, expecting it to be the sole restraint against murder; fear of God is understood here as a moral faculty that governs behavior toward others.
The angel commands Hagar not to fear, addressing the inner distress she feels in her desperate situation; God's hearing of the child counters the fear with assurance of care.
Abraham's willingness to sacrifice his son is taken as proof that he fears God—fear here is an inner disposition of reverent obedience that overrides even the deepest natural attachment.
Isaac's fear of being killed on account of Rebekah's beauty drives him to deceive; the inner state directly controls speech and shapes the will toward self-protective deception.
God tells Isaac not to fear, grounding the command in his own presence and promise of blessing—the fear addressed is an inner anxiety about security, displaced by divine companionship.
Isaac trembles violently upon realizing the blessing was given to the wrong son—the trembling is an involuntary bodily expression of inner shock and profound distress at an irreversible act.
Isaac's great trembling names the intensity of his inner shock when he discovers he has been deceived; the trembling registers the inner disruption caused by the loss of control over the blessing.
Jacob is afraid upon waking from the dream-vision; the fear is awe before a holy place recognized as the house of God, combining reverence and alarm at unexpected divine nearness.
Jacob fled secretly because he feared Laban would seize his wives by force; the inner state is an anticipatory dread of coercion that governs his decision-making and prompts flight.
The 'Fear of Isaac' is used as a divine name, suggesting that dread of God was the defining inner orientation of Isaac's life—a reverent awe that characterizes his relationship with God.
Jacob swears by the Fear of his father Isaac, invoking the awesome God whom Isaac revered; dread of God serves here as the inner ground of solemn oath and covenant commitment.
Jacob is greatly afraid and distressed at the prospect of meeting Esau; the fear is an intense inner alarm that immediately drives strategic action—splitting his people into two camps.
Jacob confesses he fears Esau, fearing violent attack on himself and his family; this inner dread is the explicit motivation for his urgent prayer for deliverance.
A God-sent terror fell upon the surrounding cities, paralyzing their will to pursue Jacob's sons; the inner state in the enemies is externally imposed dread that disables hostile action.
The midwife commands Rachel not to fear during the hardest moment of childbirth; the fear addressed is an acute, pain-driven inner dread in the face of mortal danger during labor.
Joseph grounds his offer of mercy in his own fear of God—fear here is an inner moral orientation that governs his treatment of others and gives him a reason not to exploit his power.

The brothers' hearts fail and they turn trembling to one another upon finding the money; trembling is the bodily and inner expression of shock and fearful bewilderment at a seemingly inexplicable event.
Both the brothers and Jacob are afraid when they see the money in every sack; the inner fear is triggered by the inexplicable return of the silver, which they perceive as ominous and threatening.
The men feel afraid on being brought into Joseph's house, immediately imagining the worst—enslavement and seizure; the inner fear generates an elaborate, anxiety-driven interpretation of events.

The steward addresses the brothers' fear directly with 'Peace to you, do not be afraid,' offering a theological explanation; the fear is an inner alarm about punishment that the steward seeks to calm.
The brothers are struck with amazement when seated in exact birth order before Joseph; the inner state is astonished bewilderment at what they cannot explain, expressed in their mutual staring.
The brothers are dismayed at Joseph's self-revelation and cannot answer him; the inner state is an overwhelming, speechless shock at the sudden collapse of their assumed reality about his fate.
God tells Jacob not to fear going to Egypt, implying Jacob's inner reluctance or apprehension about the journey; divine promise of nationhood is offered as the ground for releasing the fear.

Joseph tells his brothers not to fear him, deflecting the assumption that he would take vengeance; the brothers' inner fear is one of anticipated punishment, which Joseph counters by deferring to God's authority.
Joseph reassures his brothers not to fear, backing the command with a promise of provision; the inner fear is anxiety about survival and retribution, dissolved by his pledge of care and kind words.
The midwives' fear of God functions as an inner moral allegiance that overrides the king's command; it is a reverent loyalty to God that controls the will even under coercive royal authority.
Because the midwives feared God, he rewarded them with families; fear of God is the inner orientation that motivates righteous disobedience and is acknowledged by God as the basis for blessing.
Moses is afraid when he realizes his killing of the Egyptian is known; the inner state is acute fear of exposure and punishment, prompting cognitive alarm—'Surely the thing is known.'
Moses hides his face because he is afraid to look at God; the inner state is reverential dread in the direct presence of the divine, expressed in a bodily act of averted gaze.

Those among Pharaoh's servants who feared God's word acted on that inner orientation immediately, protecting their dependents; the fear functions as a disposition that produces urgent, responsible action.
Moses tells Pharaoh and his servants they do not yet fear the Lord God; the absence of this inner disposition is the reason they will not genuinely relent, despite the plagues.
Moses' hot anger as he leaves Pharaoh is the intense inner burning of righteous indignation; it marks the emotional intensity of his inner state after delivering God's final warning.
Israel fears greatly when they see the Egyptian army approaching; the inner state is intense, acute alarm in the face of visible mortal danger, prompting them to cry out to God.
Moses commands Israel not to fear as the Egyptians close in; fear is an inner impulse toward panic and flight that Moses counters by redirecting attention to God's imminent saving action.
After witnessing God's power over Egypt, Israel feared the Lord and believed; the fear shifts from dread of Egypt to reverent awe of God, producing faith as its inner companion.
God is celebrated as awesome in glorious deeds; the awe here is the proper inner response evoked by God's incomparable holiness and mighty acts, framing worship as wonder-filled reverence.

The nations tremble upon hearing of what God did at the sea; the trembling is an involuntary inner and bodily response of dread produced by hearing of overwhelming divine power directed against enemies.
The chiefs of Edom are dismayed by news of Israel's deliverance; the inner state is a sudden, overwhelming alarm that collapses their confidence and leaves leaders of surrounding nations undone.
Trembling seizes the leaders of Moab upon hearing of God's acts; the trembling is an uncontrollable inner and physical response to news of divine power, paralyzing those who hear it.
Terror falls upon the nations as Israel passes through; the terror is an externally imposed inner state of dread caused by God's overwhelming power, rendering enemies motionless as stone.
Dread accompanies terror in falling upon the nations; paired with terror, it describes the paralyzing inner grip of fear that immobilizes enemies before God's mighty arm on Israel's behalf.

The hardship Israel endured on their journey is recalled as a shared ordeal — a burden of suffering experienced collectively and now recounted to Jethro, marking its weight on those who lived through it.
Fearing God is named here as an inner moral quality that qualifies men for leadership — it is paired with trustworthiness and integrity, showing it shapes reliable, just character from within.
The overwhelming sensory phenomena at Sinai — thunder, lightning, thick cloud, loud trumpet — produced involuntary trembling in all the people, a whole-body fear response to divine presence.
The mountain itself trembled greatly at the Lord's fiery descent — the term here is applied to the physical world shaking in response to divine presence, extending the register beyond the human body.
Moses distinguishes panic-fear (do not fear) from reverential fear — the God-given fear that stays before them as a moral restraint against sin, an inward disposition kept continually in view.

Reverential fear of God is described as something that stands before the person — a persistent inward orientation God intends to place in them through the Sinai encounter, functioning as a guard against sin.

God's terror is sent as an advance weapon — it works on enemy peoples' inner being, throwing them into confusion and causing them to flee, so that it is God's dread, not Israel's strength, that defeats them.

The awesome work God will do provokes a fearful reverence — his unparalleled marvels before all nations produce the inner recognition that Israel's God is terrifyingly great, making him worthy of awe.

The radiance of Moses' face after encountering God produced fear in Aaron and the people — an involuntary, awe-driven recoil that kept them from approaching him, showing fear directed at reflected divine glory.
Revering father and mother is commanded alongside keeping Sabbaths, grounding filial reverence in the same inner disposition of fear owed to God — an ethical obligation rooted in reverential awe.

Fearing God is the inner constraint that prevents exploiting the vulnerable — the prohibition against harming the deaf and blind is backed not by human oversight but by God-directed reverence within the person.
Reverencing the sanctuary is an outward act rooted in an inner disposition of holy awe — paired with Sabbath-keeping, it reflects a reverence that shapes how one relates to sacred space and time.
Fearing God is the inward motivation for honoring the elderly — the reverence owed God is the ground from which respect for human dignity flows, linking inner awe to outward ethical action.
Fear of God is named as the inner foundation for standing before the aged and honoring them — reverence toward the divine extends outward into respect for persons, especially the vulnerable elderly.
Fearing God is the inward check that prevents wronging one's neighbor in business dealings — where no human court can see, God-directed reverence within the person is the operative moral restraint.

Fear of God functions as the interior judge in economic life — the command not to wrong one another is enforced by an inner reverence before God who sees all, substituting for external legal accountability.
Fearing God is the inner principle that restrains profiting from a brother's poverty — reverence toward God motivates generosity and forbids exploiting a neighbor's vulnerability for financial gain.

The fear of God is named as the alternative to taking interest from an impoverished brother — it is the inward reverence that produces the will to let one's brother live beside you without exploitation.
Fear of God is the inner force that prohibits ruthless domination of a servant — it is the reverential awareness of God's authority that checks the impulse to abuse power over another person.
The command to fear God stands as the sole restraint against ruling a fellow Israelite ruthlessly — inner awe before God is positioned as the only adequate check on the will to dominate another.

Reverencing the sanctuary is coupled with Sabbath observance as paired expressions of inner awe — both are outward signs of the reverence Israel is to maintain toward God and the sacred order he established.

The absence of trembling is promised as the fruit of covenant peace — God's gift of security means no one will make them afraid, so the lack of fear-trembling marks flourishing life under divine protection.
God rebukes Miriam and Aaron for speaking against Moses without fear — the question implies that the intimacy God shares with Moses should have produced reverential awe restraining their presumption.

Caleb and Joshua urge the people not to fear the Canaanites — the enemies' protection is removed, so fear of them is unreasonable; true reverence belongs to the Lord who is with Israel, not to human opponents.

Israel's hardship in the wilderness is invoked as shared historical knowledge in a diplomatic appeal — the suffering endured on the journey is a lived burden that warrants Edom's sympathy and passage.
God directly commands Moses not to fear Og — the divine assurance that the enemy is already given into Israel's hand counters the inward dread that the formidable king might otherwise produce.

Moab's dread of Israel is visceral and overwhelming — the great number of the people produced an intense inward terror in the Moabites, described as being overcome, showing fear as a paralyzing inner state.
Judges are forbidden to be intimidated by any person — the inner shrinking before powerful litigants must not distort justice, because the judgment belongs to God, not to any human authority.
Israel is commanded not to fear or be dismayed as they take possession of the land — the prohibition addresses the inner paralysis that would prevent obedient action when facing the challenge ahead.
Moses tells Israel not to dread or be afraid of their enemies — fear of human opponents is contrasted implicitly with trust in God, and the command targets the inward dread that disables action.
The people are told not to be in dread — the term names the inner, overwhelming terror that grips a person before a feared enemy; here it is prohibited because God himself fights for Israel.
The Edomites will be afraid of Israel as they pass through — their fear is an inward, spontaneous response to Israel's presence and reputation, creating a vulnerability Israel must handle with care.

Fear of Israel is placed by God on the nations — it is an inner dread God himself instills, producing trembling and anguish in those who merely hear Israel's report, demonstrating divine control of nations' inner states.

God promises to put dread of Israel on all peoples under heaven — this God-sent dread seizes the inner being of distant nations upon hearing Israel's name, producing trembling and anguish as its effects.
Nations under heaven will tremble and writhe in anguish at the report of Israel — the trembling is the bodily-inner expression of an overwhelming, God-sent dread that hearing Israel's name provokes.

God tells Moses not to fear Og king of Bashan — the divine word directly addresses and counters the inward fear that the king's formidable reputation would naturally produce, replacing it with divine assurance.

Israel shall not fear their enemies because the Lord fights for them — the prohibition targets the inward fear that would undermine resolve, grounding the absence of fear entirely in God's active intervention.

Fear of God is the outcome God intends from the Horeb encounter — hearing his words was meant to produce a lasting inward fear that would be taught to children and sustained all the days of their lives.

God's great deeds of terror in Egypt are acts that overwhelm the will — the term names the fear-inspiring power displayed through wonders and war that demonstrated God's unmatched authority over nations.

The great deeds of terror performed in Egypt are cited as unique in all of history — this fear-producing power of God, witnessed personally by Israel, grounds the claim that no other god has acted comparably.

Israel's fear of the fire at Sinai kept them from ascending the mountain — the inner terror produced by the visible divine fire was so intense it drove them back, requiring Moses to mediate in their place.

God longs for Israel to have a heart always disposed to fear him — this reverence is located in the heart, seen as the seat of enduring moral orientation, producing obedience and generational flourishing.

Fearing the Lord is the inward orientation that expresses itself in keeping all his statutes — it is to be transmitted across generations and sustains long life, making it the root of both piety and blessing.

Fearing the Lord is the exclusive orientation of Israel's inner allegiance — it is paired with service and oath-taking, showing that reverential fear properly directed toward God shapes the whole of loyal life.

Fearing the Lord is commanded as an ongoing disposition for Israel's own good — it is the inward posture that produces obedience, and God's purpose in requiring it is Israel's preservation and flourishing.

Israel is told not to be afraid of the nations they must displace — the inward dread of numerous enemies is to be replaced by memory of what God did to Pharaoh, grounding courage in historical experience.

Israel feared the nations before them, but God promises to act the same way toward those peoples as he did to Egypt — their fear of human enemies is addressed by pointing to God's demonstrated power as the greater reality.

Israel must not dread the nations because the Lord, a great and awesome God, dwells in their midst — the inward dread of enemies is countered by the nearer reality of divine presence, which is itself more awe-inspiring.

Israel is commanded not to be filled with dread at the nations — the term names the inner trembling before overwhelming enemies, and its prohibition is grounded in God's own awesome presence among them.
Fearing God is the inward disposition that issues in walking in his ways — it is named alongside commandment-keeping as the spiritual root from which obedient conduct flows in daily life.
Moses feared the Lord's burning anger directed at Israel, an inner dread of divine wrath so intense it moved him to urgent intercessory prayer on their behalf.
Fearing the Lord is presented as the foundational inner orientation God requires, expressed outwardly through walking in his ways, loving him, and serving him with the whole heart and soul.
The term 'awesome' describes God's character as one who inspires reverential dread; his impartiality and incorruptibility ground that awe rather than any capricious power.
Fearing the Lord is commanded as the defining inner posture that should shape all conduct—service, loyalty, and oath-taking—toward God.
The 'terrifying things' God has done are witnessed firsthand; this objective divine power seen by Israel's own eyes is the basis for reverential awe toward him.
Fear of Israel is divinely imposed on the surrounding peoples; it is an external, territory-wide dread that paralyzes resistance and ensures no enemy can stand against them.
This dread of Israel, paired with fear, is God-sent upon all the land; it functions as a conquering inner force in enemies that prevents any effective opposition.
Dread of Israel is God's promised gift to his people, placed in their enemies' hearts so that no one can withstand them as they advance into the land.
Fearing God is the inner anchor of a whole way of life, paired with obedience and loyalty; it is the motivating disposition from which all faithful conduct flows.
Public execution of an idolater produces fear in all Israel; this socially instilled inner dread of divine judgment functions as a deterrent against repeating such wickedness.
Regular worship practices at the chosen sanctuary are designed to cultivate an enduring inner reverence toward God—fear is something that must be learned and sustained over time.
Public punishment of presumptuous wrongdoing instills fear throughout the community, curbing arrogance; the inner restraint produced by hearing of judgment prevents future defiance.
Daily reading of the law is prescribed so the king may learn an ongoing inner reverence toward God, rooted in careful obedience rather than power or privilege.
When a prophet's word fails, the people need not feel any inner dread toward him; the absence of divine confirmation strips the false prophet of any fear-inducing authority.
Punishment of a false witness instills fear in the rest of the community; the inner deterrent effect of witnessed justice restrains others from committing similar evil.
Israel is commanded not to fear a vastly superior enemy army; the inner terror that overwhelming odds would naturally provoke is countered by the assurance of God's presence.
The priest commands soldiers not to let fear grip them before battle; it is an inner response of the heart that must be consciously resisted to prevent collapse of courage.
Trembling in dread of the enemy is explicitly forbidden before battle; it is an inner physical-emotional response that would undermine the soldiers' resolve and must be checked.
The fearful and fainthearted soldier whose inner terror might spread to others is sent home; his fear is contagious and capable of melting the courage of his fellow fighters.
Public stoning of a rebellious son produces fear throughout all Israel; the communal inner response of dread serves as a moral and social restraint on similar lawlessness.
The Amalekites' attack on the weak is evidence they did not fear God; absence of inner reverence toward God removed all moral restraint and made cruelty possible.
The Exodus is characterized by 'great deeds of terror'—acts that inspired overwhelming dread; the fear produced was the experiential impact of God's mighty intervention in history.
God's outstretched arm in the Exodus produced awe-inspiring terror; this fear is the inner impact of witnessing divine power overwhelming all human and natural resistance.
When nations see Israel bearing the Lord's name, they will be afraid; the fear is a divinely induced inner response in outsiders provoked by God's visible identification with his people.
Confusion of mind is a divine curse striking the inner faculty of cognition; the bewilderment disables rational perception and leaves the person unable to make sense of reality.
Careful obedience to the law is linked to fearing God's glorious and awesome name; reverence is the inner motivating disposition that careful Torah-keeping is meant to express.
Fearing the glorious and awesome name of the Lord is set as the inner goal of Torah observance; failure to cultivate that reverence through obedience is the root of covenant unfaithfulness.
Israel's former terror of Egypt's diseases is recalled; God will bring back the very afflictions they once dreaded, exploiting that deep-seated fear as part of the covenant curse.
A trembling heart is given by God as a curse in exile—an unceasing inner agitation that denies rest, paired with failing eyes and a languishing soul, describing total inner collapse.
Continual dread without assurance of life is a covenantal curse; the inner experience is unrelenting, consuming both night and day, with no relief or certainty of survival.
Dread is explicitly located in the heart and tied to what the eyes see; it produces such unbearable inner anguish that the sufferer constantly wishes the present moment away.
This dread is seated in the heart and driven by terrifying sights; the inner experience is so overwhelming it distorts the experience of time, making each hour intolerable.
Fear of enemies is prohibited and countered by God's covenant presence; the inner dread that enemies might provoke is displaced by the assurance that God will not abandon his people.
Trembling before enemies is forbidden alongside fear; God's presence is the reason the inner urge to shrink in dread need not control the soldier's response.
Joshua is commanded not to fear or be dismayed; God's going before and accompanying him is the ground for overcoming the inner instinct to dread what lies ahead.
Public assembly for Torah reading is aimed at cultivating learned fear of God; reverence is not innate but is formed in the community through regular hearing and practice.
Children who have not yet heard the law are to be taught it so they too may learn to fear God; reverence is a formative inner orientation that must be transmitted across generations.
Terror is depicted as an indoor killer, matching the sword outdoors; it strikes across all ages and both sexes, portraying an all-consuming dread that destroys life from within sheltered spaces.
God refrains from full destruction of Israel partly out of dread of how enemies would misinterpret it; this inner apprehension about enemy pride shapes his restrained judgment.
Moses's great deeds of terror are his defining legacy; the awe-inspiring fear produced in those who witnessed God's power through him marks him as unparalleled among prophets.
The terror-producing deeds Moses performed before all Israel define his unmatched prophetic ministry; the inner dread these acts inspired was the experiential sign of divine power at work.
Joshua is commanded not to be frightened or dismayed; God's universal presence with him is the explicit reason the inner urge to tremble in dread should not take hold.
Rahab reports that the fear of Israel has already fallen upon Canaan's inhabitants; this divinely induced terror causes the people to melt inwardly, dissolving their capacity to resist.
All Israel stood in awe of Joshua as they had of Moses; this reverential fear arose from witnessing God's exaltation of Joshua, establishing his authority through experienced wonder.
God's mighty acts at the Jordan are intended to produce enduring fear of the Lord in Israel; the inner reverence flows from direct experience of divine power displayed in history.
God commands Joshua not to fear before the battle of Ai; the divine guarantee of victory is the stated reason the inner response of dread should not govern his will or action.
The Gibeonites feared greatly for their lives in response to God's command to destroy Canaan's inhabitants; this existential dread of death drove their deceptive self-preservation strategy.
Adoni-zedek feared greatly because Gibeon's defection to Israel posed a massive strategic threat; this inner dread of the military consequences motivated his urgent coalition-building response.
God directly prohibits Joshua from fearing the five Amorite kings; the divine promise to deliver all enemies into his hand is the ground for overcoming inner dread before battle.
Joshua commands his officers not to be afraid or dismayed after the victory; the Lord's pattern of defeating enemies is presented as the basis for displacing inner fear with courage.
God commands Joshua's inner dread of the enemy to stop, grounding the release of fear in a divine promise of military victory delivered tomorrow.
The anxiety here is a forward-looking worry in the will — the altar-builders acted preemptively out of dread that future generations would be cut off from the Lord.
The fear of exclusion from worship is the inner object — the dread that children might be told they have no share in the Lord and thus cease to revere him.
Fear of the Lord here is commanded as an interior orientation of the whole person expressed outwardly in sincere service and faithfulness, contrasted with serving other gods.
Jael addresses Sisera's apprehensive inner state directly, commanding it to stop so he will enter and trust her — his fear of pursuit is the felt danger being suppressed.
The prohibition targets misplaced inner reverence — Israel was commanded not to let the Amorite gods occupy the place of awe in their hearts, yet they disobeyed.
Gideon's terror at having seen the Lord face-to-face is directly addressed; God's peace-greeting cancels the dread of death that the theophany had produced in him.
Gideon's fear of social retaliation from his household and townsmen is an inner restraint that overrides daytime courage, driving him to act secretly at night.
Trembling here is an involuntary physical-inner state that disqualifies a soldier for battle; it is paired with fearfulness and signals a will unable to hold steady under threat.
Being fearful is an inner disposition that renders soldiers unfit for Gideon's campaign; those who possess it are dismissed, leaving only those whose inner resolve holds.
Fear is treated here as a possible inner impediment to obedient action; if it exists, God provides an alternative path — going with a companion — that accommodates the weakness.
The panic thrown into Midian's army is an outwardly visible collapse of inner resolve — the capture of their kings triggers a contagious terror that disintegrates their will to fight.
Jether's fear is a youth's inner inhibition — his age prevents his will from overriding the dread, so he cannot execute the commanded killing despite his father's order.
The angel's appearance produced an overwhelming sense of the awesome in the woman — the divine presence registered in her perception as terrifying and beyond ordinary human address.
Benjamin's dismay is an inner collapse triggered by perceiving imminent disaster; it moves from perception to panic, breaking their capacity to continue fighting.
Boaz is startled from sleep by an unexpected physical presence — an involuntary inner jolt of alarm at discovering a woman lying at his feet in the dark.
Boaz reassures Ruth's anxious inner state, grounding the release of fear in his intention to act and in the community's already-established knowledge of her good character.
Samuel's fear is a reluctance of will rooted in dread of delivering a hard divine word — the night vision's severity made him shrink from telling Eli what God had said.
The Philistines' fear is a recognition in their minds that a divine power has entered the Israelite camp — they perceive the ark's arrival as a lethal threat and cry out in dread.
Eli's trembling is located explicitly in his heart — a deep inner anxiety for the ark's safety that precedes and outlasts the battle report, seated in his core loyalty to God.
The women's 'do not be afraid' addresses a dying mother's inner anguish; yet her grief over Israel's disaster is so total that the reassurance of a newborn son cannot reach her.
Israel's fear of the Philistines is an inner response to military threat that drives them to Samuel; the fear functions as the impulse behind their cry for divine help.
The dread of the Lord is an external force that falls upon the people, overriding their individual wills and producing unanimous, immediate military mobilisation.
Fearing the Lord is presented as the foundational interior disposition from which obedience, service, and loyalty to God's commands must all flow.
Thunder and rain produce a great inner fear of the Lord in all the people — a sensory divine act reshapes their inner attitude from complacency into reverent awe.
Samuel dismisses the people's fear even in the face of acknowledged sin, redirecting their inner state away from paralysing dread toward wholehearted continued service.
Fear of the Lord is commanded as an all-heart devotion grounded in memory of God's great deeds — it is the inner orientation from which faithful service must arise.
The people's trembling as they follow Saul is a physical expression of inner terror — the military crisis has so shaken their composure that their bodies manifest the fear.
Trembling spreads through the Philistine garrison as a contagious inner panic; joined by an earthquake, it produces a total collapse of military cohesion described as a great panic.
The trembling is a camp-wide inner panic, spreading through field and garrison alike and intensified by the earthquake — a collective inner terror that escalates into full rout.
The people's fear here is directed at a sworn oath — their inner dread of violating Saul's curse overrides hunger and prevents any hand from taking the honey.
Saul confesses that fear of the people was the inner motive behind his disobedience — human social dread displaced his fear of God and drove him to comply with the crowd.
The elders' trembling at Samuel's arrival is an inner apprehension of danger — his unexpected presence stirs fear of divine judgment, expressed bodily before he even speaks.
Saul and all Israel are simultaneously dismayed and deeply afraid — Goliath's words produce a two-layered inner impact: shock that disorganises thought and fear that disables action.
The sight of Goliath produces extreme fear that immediately overrides the will — all the men of Israel flee before the threat without deliberation, driven by overwhelming dread.
Saul's fear of David is rooted in theological perception — he recognises that God's presence has transferred from himself to David, and that recognition generates a deep dread.
Saul's dread of David is an awe-laden fear triggered by David's extraordinary success — he recognises a power in David that exceeds himself and responds with fearful reverence.
Saul's fear of David intensifies and becomes permanent — it is the inner root of his sustained enmity, showing how dread can harden into ongoing hostile intent.
Jonathan's burning anger is a fierce inner heat of indignation ignited by his father's shaming of David — the emotion is so intense it expels appetite and consumes the day.
Ahimelech's trembling at David's solitary arrival is an inner alarm — the unexpected, unaccompanied appearance of a prominent figure signals danger and unsettles his composure.
David takes threatening words to heart — the inner reception of intelligence about his reputation among enemies produces a strong, located fear directed at Achish specifically.
David dismisses Abiathar's fear by reframing their shared danger — since the same enemy threatens both, staying together is the solution, and fear need not drive the fugitive away.
David's men name an existing inner fear — they already feel unsafe in Judah and reason that going into greater danger would compound a dread they are already carrying.
Jonathan addresses David's fear with assurance grounded in divine promise — Saul's hand will not reach him, and the future kingship Jonathan names is intended to displace dread with hope.
The term names a staggering of conscience — Abigail warns that unnecessary bloodshed would produce an inner stumbling, a moral unsteadiness that would haunt David's future reign.
Saul's trembling is explicitly located in his heart — the Philistine army's size produces a deep inner shaking, a physical-emotional quaking that the text seats at the centre of his person.
Fear and heart-trembling are paired — Saul's visual perception of the enemy army triggers an inner collapse of courage, with the emotion immediately registered in his heart.
The medium is seized with terror at the apparition she sees; Saul's 'do not be afraid' is an attempt to stabilise her inner panic so she can give him the information he needs.
Saul's distress is the reason he has disturbed Samuel — inner anguish born of divine silence and military crisis drives him to desperate measures, seeking guidance from the dead.
Fear from Samuel's words fills Saul entirely and prostrates him — the inner terror is so overwhelming it drains all physical strength, leaving him collapsed and unable to eat.
Saul's inner being was visibly shattered by terror — the woman saw he was terrified, prompting her compassionate response; the dismay was so total it was written on his face and body.
The armor-bearer's fear of killing the Lord's anointed king was so overwhelming it overrode a direct command, paralyzing his will and preventing action even to spare Saul from worse suffering.
The agony gripping the wounded man is an internal seizure — it has taken hold of him body and soul, making death preferable to the torment of lingering between life and death.

David's rebuke frames the absence of fear as a moral failure — the messenger lacked the reverential dread that should have prevented him from touching God's anointed, exposing a void in his inner conscience.
Ish-bosheth's fear of Abner was so complete that it shut down his capacity to speak — the emotion seized his will and silenced him entirely, leaving him unable to respond.
Abner's death dismayed all Israel — the collective inner collapse is pictured as courage failing, showing that the nation's confidence and resolve dissolved when their military strongman was gone.
After Uzzah's death, David feared the Lord — a dread so acute it redirected his will, making him question whether the ark could safely come to him and halting his intended action.
The verse frames future security as the absence of inner disturbance — God's people will be planted so they are no longer unsettled or shaken by violent oppressors as they had been before.
The term describes the awesome, fear-inspiring nature of God's great acts done for Israel — the deeds themselves produce a sense of overwhelming dread and wonder in those who witness them.

David's command 'do not fear' addresses Mephibosheth's evident inner dread of the powerful king — David redirects that fear by replacing threat with promise of kindness, targeting the will and emotions.
Military defeat produced a fear that reshaped the Syrians' political will — dread of Israel was strong enough to make them abandon their alliance with Ammon and submit to Israel instead.
David's servants feared his reaction to news of the child's death — anxiety about how he might harm himself paralyzed their will and kept them silent, showing fear controlling decision and speech.
Absalom commands his servants not to fear before killing Amnon — the command acknowledges that fear would naturally inhibit their will to act, and he overrides it by invoking his authority.
The woman says the people made her afraid — this fear acted as the inner pressure that finally drove her will to approach the king, showing how dread can paradoxically motivate action.
Ahithophel plans to strike David when he is weary and throw him into panic — exploiting the moment when inner fear would collapse his followers' resolve and cause them to flee without a fight.
The king was deeply moved — grief shook David's inner being violently, driving him to weeping and a cry of anguish that wished his own death instead of his son's.
The waves of death are described as encompassing the psalmist — the imagery of overwhelming breakers pressing in from all sides captures the total engulfment of the inner person by mortal terror.
The fear of God is presented as the defining orientation of a just ruler — it is the inner governing principle that aligns the will toward righteous rule rather than self-interest or cruelty.
Adonijah's guests trembled at news of Solomon's anointing — the fear seized them suddenly and physically, scattering the assembly as each fled individually, showing panic breaking collective resolve.
Adonijah's fear of Solomon drove him to the altar for sanctuary — the dread of the new king's power redirected his will entirely, from rival claimant to one seeking mercy and protection.

Adonijah's fear of Solomon is reported as the reason he grasped the altar — the inner dread of being executed expressed itself in a public act of submission, linking fear directly to self-preserving behavior.
All Israel stood in awe of Solomon after his judgment — perceiving divine wisdom in him produced a reverential fear that combined admiration with recognition of an authority beyond the merely human.
Solomon prays that the people may fear God all their days in the promised land — fear here is a sustained inner orientation of reverence that should govern the whole of life in relationship to God.
Solomon prays that foreigners who hear of God may come to fear him as Israel does — the desired fear is an inner knowing of God's name that shapes allegiance and worship across all peoples.

Elijah's 'do not fear' addresses the widow's natural terror in the face of starvation — the command calls her to override that inner dread and act in trust, prioritizing Elijah's word above her anxiety.

Obadiah's fearing the Lord greatly is presented as his defining character — it shaped his identity and conduct in Ahab's household, including his courageous protection of the prophets at personal risk.
Obadiah appeals to his lifelong fear of the Lord as moral credentials — this deep-seated reverence from youth formed his inner character and distinguished him from Ahab's godless household.
The angel commands Elijah not to be afraid of the king — addressing the natural fear that might have made Elijah hesitate, the divine word reassures him so his will can comply without shrinking back.
The widow identifies her dead husband by his fear of the Lord — this reverence was the core of his inner character, a quality so defining that it serves as his chief moral credential before Elisha.
Elisha counters his servant's fear by pointing to superior heavenly forces — the 'do not be afraid' addresses inner terror, redirecting perception from visible threat to invisible divine protection.

The officials were exceedingly afraid of Jehu — their fear so overwhelmed them that they reasoned from it: if two kings couldn't withstand him, they had no hope, and fear paralyzed any will to resist.

Israel's fearing other gods is named as the root sin behind their exile — the inner allegiance of reverential devotion was wrongly directed away from the Lord and toward idols, corrupting their fundamental orientation.

Israel's fearing other gods is the spiritual defection at the heart of the nation's downfall — the same reverential inner posture owed to God was given instead to false gods, constituting the primary offense.
The new settlers did not fear the Lord, and lion attacks followed — the absence of reverential fear is presented as a dangerous void in the inner life that drew divine judgment through natural means.

A priest was sent to teach the settlers how to fear the Lord — this shows that proper reverential fear is a learned, instructed inner orientation, not instinctive, requiring formation through priestly teaching.

The settlers feared the Lord externally through appointed priests and ritual, yet the fear was mixed with other gods — showing an inner religious orientation that was divided and therefore not genuine covenant fear.

They feared the Lord but simultaneously served their own gods — the inner double allegiance reveals that their fear was superficial, coexisting with competing loyalties rather than defining exclusive devotion.

True fear of the Lord is defined by obedience to his statutes and commandments — the verse shows that fearing God is not a feeling alone but an inner orientation expressed through faithful covenant keeping.

God's covenant command forbids fearing other gods — the prohibition targets the inner allegiance of reverential devotion, showing that worship and fear belong exclusively to the Lord by covenant obligation.

Fear of the Lord is commanded and grounded in his saving act from Egypt — the inner reverence is to be directed solely to the one who demonstrated his power, and expressed through bowing and sacrifice.

Fearing other gods is prohibited alongside careful obedience to God's written commands — the verse pairs inner reverence with outward obedience, showing fear of God must translate into consistent covenantal conduct.

The prohibition against fearing other gods is linked to not forgetting the covenant — correct inner fear is inseparable from memory and loyalty; forsaking the covenant opens the heart to misplaced reverence.

Fear of the Lord your God is paired with the promise of deliverance from all enemies — the inner orientation of reverence toward God is presented as the condition upon which protection and security rest.

These nations feared the Lord and served carved images simultaneously — the split inner allegiance passed down through generations, showing that syncretistic fear is not true fear but a persistent compromise.

Isaiah commands Hezekiah's servants not to be afraid of Assyria's threatening words — the fear addressed is an inward dread triggered by enemy speech, and God's word through Isaiah is given to counter it.
Gedaliah's oath aims to dispel the people's fear of Chaldean officials — he urges them to settle and serve rather than flee, addressing an anxiety that was driving them toward dangerous decisions.
Fear of the Chaldeans drove all the people — great and small — to abandon the land for Egypt; the dread was a collective force that overrode reason and covenant faithfulness, producing mass flight.
The armor-bearer's great fear prevented him from killing Saul despite a direct command — reverent dread of slaying the Lord's anointed paralyzed his will entirely, forcing Saul to take his own life.
David's fear of God after Uzzah's death was immediate and profound — it arose from witnessing divine judgment and redirected his will, making him question his fitness to bring the ark home.

The Lord placed dread of David upon all surrounding nations — this externally imposed fear was not David's own achievement but a divine act that shaped the inner posture of foreign peoples toward Israel's king.
The Lord's incomparable greatness makes him uniquely worthy of reverent awe, placing him above all rival gods as the proper object of fear-as-worship.
God promises Israel freedom from the inner and outer disturbance caused by violent oppressors, so that the people will no longer be shaken or unsettled in their dwelling.
God's acts of redemption and nation-driving are described as great and awesome things, evoking reverent dread of his power on behalf of his people.
Fearfulness is explicitly contrasted with strength and courage; the command not to fear calls Solomon to inner resolve grounded in obedience to God's statutes.
The command not to be afraid is grounded in God's presence and faithfulness; fear is an inner state that could paralyze the work, countered by the assurance of divine accompaniment.
The goal of answered prayer for foreigners is that they may come to fear God as Israel does—reverence as the inner posture that aligns the nations with God's people.
The fear of the Lord fell upon the surrounding cities as an overpowering force that paralyzed their resistance, enabling Judah to plunder them without opposition.
The dread of the Lord fell upon surrounding kingdoms as a restraining force on their wills, preventing them from making war against Jehoshaphat.
The fear of the Lord is summoned as an inward ethical constraint upon judges, guarding against injustice, partiality, and bribery in their decisions.
The fear of the Lord is the governing inner disposition from which faithful, wholehearted judicial conduct is to flow, paired explicitly with faithfulness and whole-heartedness.
Dread of God came upon all surrounding nations when they heard of his direct intervention in battle, overwhelming their willingness to oppose Israel.
The dismissed troops returned home in fierce burning anger—an intense, consuming inner heat of rage directed at Judah for their discharge.
The Assyrians shouted loudly in Judah's language specifically to terrify the defenders on the wall, weaponizing dismay to break their will and surrender the city.
Terror of the surrounding peoples pressed upon the returned exiles, yet their fear drove them toward God rather than away—they built the altar and offered sacrifices despite the threat.
External enemies deliberately induced fear in the builders of Judah, targeting their inner resolve to halt construction by making them afraid to continue.
Trembling at God's words describes an inner posture of solemn dread in response to Israel's unfaithfulness—those who held this disposition gathered around Ezra in shared appalled grief.
Trembling at God's commandment is the inner disposition that qualifies certain leaders as reliable counselors in the covenant decision to put away foreign wives.
The assembled people trembled from two sources simultaneously—the moral gravity of the matter of unfaithfulness and the physical distress of heavy rain—showing bodily and inner agitation combined.
God is addressed as great and awesome, establishing reverent awe as the fitting inner posture before the covenant-keeping God to whom Nehemiah prays.
Fearing God's name is described as something the servants delight in—a willing, joyful inner devotion rather than reluctant dread, offered as the basis of Nehemiah's petition.
Nehemiah was very much afraid before the king—a fear seated in the heart, visible in his countenance, arising from the danger of appearing sad before a powerful ruler.
Fear of human enemies is countered by calling the people to remember the great and awesome Lord—redirecting inner dread toward trust and courageous action.
The awesome greatness of the Lord is invoked as the antidote to fear of enemies; reverent awe of God is set against terror of opponents as the motivating force for the people's defense.
Fear of God is the inner ethical standard that should have restrained the leaders from oppressing their own people, with the nations' contempt used as a shaming motive.
Fear of God operated as an inner moral restraint in Nehemiah, preventing him from abusing his authority as previous governors had done by burdening the people.
Enemies sought to induce fear in Nehemiah's workers to weaken their will and cause their hands to drop from the work—fear as a weapon targeting resolve and agency.
Enemies hired a false prophet to induce fear in Nehemiah, hoping that fear would drive him to sin and disgrace himself—fear targeted as a tool to corrupt his will.
Tobiah and Sanballat, along with the prophetess Noadiah, used intimidation to make Nehemiah afraid—a sustained campaign to undermine his inner steadiness.
When the enemies heard the wall was finished, they were afraid and their self-esteem collapsed—recognizing God's hand produced a fear that deflated their inner confidence.
Tobiah's letters were deliberately sent to make Nehemiah afraid—an external psychological pressure designed to shake his inner resolve through intimidation.
God-fearing is named as a defining character trait that qualifies Hananiah for leadership over Jerusalem—reverence for God as an enduring inner disposition of faithfulness.
God is addressed as awesome in the context of a long catalogue of national suffering, establishing reverent awe of his greatness as the frame within which Israel appeals for mercy.
The accumulated hardship of generations—kings, priests, prophets, and all the people—is laid before God as a burden too great to be treated lightly, spanning from Assyria to the present.
Dread of the Jews fell so heavily on surrounding peoples that many converted to Judaism—fear of human power producing a decisive change of communal identity and allegiance.
The dread of the Jews had fallen upon all peoples, so paralyzing opposition that no one could resist them—fear as a force that neutralized the will of their enemies.
Dread of Mordecai fell upon provincial officials and prompted them to actively assist the Jews, showing fear as a force that redirected the will of those in authority.
Fearing God is paired with blamelessness, uprightness, and turning from evil, presenting it as a comprehensive inner moral orientation that shapes all of Job's character.
God himself affirms that Job fears God and turns from evil, presenting this reverent disposition as a distinctive character quality unmatched among humans on earth.
Satan questions whether Job's fear of God is truly inner and disinterested, implying that fear-as-reverence is only meaningful when not motivated by personal reward.
God again upholds Job's God-fearing character after intense suffering, showing that genuine reverence is a disposition that holds fast even under severe trial.
Job names a dreaded thing that already sat within him as an anticipated fear, and that dread has now materialized into reality—showing fear as an inner anticipatory anguish preceding loss.
Job's dread of a specific disaster has come true, showing that dread was an inward anticipatory terror that corresponded to actual suffering—fear and event collapse into one.
The thing Job dreaded most has come upon him—dread here is an inner anticipatory terror that preceded and now corresponds to actual catastrophe, confirming its worst foreboding.
When suffering touched Job personally, dismay overtook him—showing that adversity reaching one's own self breaks inner composure in a way advising others could not prepare one for.
Eliphaz argues that Job's fear of God should itself be his confidence and hope—making reverential fear the inner foundation from which assurance and security ought to spring.
Dread came upon Eliphaz as a sudden, overwhelming experience in a night vision, producing physical trembling that shook all his bones—a total somatic and inner seizure of terror.
Dread seized Eliphaz in a visionary encounter, paired with trembling that shook his whole body—the inner experience of terror made physically tangible through uncontrollable shaking.
Trembling accompanied the dread in Eliphaz's vision, physically expressing the inner terror by shaking all his bones—the body becoming the outward register of overwhelming inner fear.
The blessed person need not fear destruction when it comes—fear of calamity is a threat to inner security that divine protection removes, freeing one from anticipatory dread.
The divinely protected person will laugh at destruction and famine rather than fear wild beasts—divine favor transforms inner terror into confident fearlessness in the face of danger.
Withholding kindness from a friend is equated with abandoning reverent fear of the Almighty, locating this fear in the inner disposition that governs how one treats others.
Job's friends are so terrified by his calamity that they become useless to him, showing terror as an inner reaction that shuts down compassionate response.
Seeing Job's suffering triggers afraid-ness in his friends that renders them ineffective, revealing fear as an inward recoil that overrides loyalty and friendship.
Job's sleepless tossing through the long night expresses inner distress and agitation—his suffering keeps his body and mind from rest until dawn.
God's dreams and visions directly terrify and dismay Job's inner being, showing that divine-sent nighttime experiences shatter his mental composure and sense of safety.
Job fears all his suffering because he believes God will not declare him innocent, showing fear rooted in the anticipation of unrelenting divine judgment.
Job pleads for God's rod to be removed so that terror of God will no longer overwhelm him, identifying divine dread as an oppressive force crushing his inner freedom to speak.
Job says he could speak freely if terror of God were removed, revealing that fear of God currently constrains his inner voice and suppresses honest self-expression.
Zophar promises that moral integrity will bring security so that Job will no longer fear, depicting freedom from fear as the inner fruit of restored innocence before God.
Zophar envisions a state of safety in which no one will make Job tremble, presenting the absence of dread-inducing threats as the mark of God-given inner security.
Job challenges his friends by asking whether God's majesty and dread would not fall upon them, presenting dread of God as an overwhelming inner weight that should compel humility.
Job asks God to withdraw his hand so that terror of him no longer terrifies, identifying divine terror as an external pressure that produces an unbearable inner state.
Job protests that God frightens him as though he were a frail driven leaf, depicting the frightening as disproportionate use of power that unmans a helpless inner sufferer.
Eliphaz accuses Job of destroying the fear of God and undermining prayerful meditation, casting fear of God as the foundational inner disposition that sustains worship and ethics.
The wicked man hears dreadful sounds in his ears even in prosperity, showing dread as an internal auditory experience haunting the conscience and anticipating destruction.
Terrors surround the wicked man on every side and pursue him relentlessly, depicting terror as an encircling, hunting force that overwhelms the inner person with no escape.
The wicked man is dragged to the king of terrors, personifying ultimate terror as a sovereign power that violently claims the one stripped of all security.
Witnesses east and west shudder with horror at the wicked man's fate, showing shuddering as a visceral, full-body inner response to witnessing catastrophic divine judgment.
Job warns his friends to dread the sword of divine judgment, urging them to take wrath's punishment seriously as a reality that should generate inward fear and self-examination.
As the sword pierces the wicked man's body, terrors come upon him, showing terror as an inner experience that assaults simultaneously with physical mortal injury.
Merely remembering his situation dismays Job in his inner being, and that dismay immediately seizes his flesh as shuddering, showing how inner terror becomes bodily trembling.
Shuddering takes hold of Job's flesh the moment memory brings dismay, showing the physical dimension of inner terror as the body involuntarily enacts what the mind perceives.
The wicked live in houses safe from dread and free from divine rod, presenting absence of fear as the (ironic) observable condition of the prosperous ungodly.
Eliphaz asks sarcastically whether God reproves Job because of Job's fear of him, using fear of God as the criterion by which divine judgment would be legitimately motivated.
Sudden terror overwhelms Job because snares surround him, depicting dismay as an inward shock that strikes without warning when external traps close in.
Eliphaz attributes sudden terror overwhelming Job to the snares around him, presenting dread as a consequence of moral entrapment that strikes the inner person like a flood.
Standing in God's presence, Job is terrified and dismayed, showing that contemplating God directly produces an overpowering inner upheaval he cannot control.
When Job considers God's presence and power, dread fills him, locating this dread in the reflective, contemplative faculty—it arises from thoughtful perception of divine greatness.
God has made Job's heart faint and the Almighty has terrified him, identifying the heart as the constitutional seat where divine action produces dismay and inner collapse.
The wicked, who love the dark, are friendly with deep-darkness terrors, showing that habitual sin reorients the inner person to be at ease with what should inspire dread.
Dread belongs to God as an attribute of his dominion, presenting fear not as a human emotion here but as an intrinsic quality of divine sovereign majesty.
Terrors overtake the wicked like a flood in the night, depicting terror as an irresistible inner onslaught that overwhelms all resistance and sweeps the person away.
Fear of the Lord is identified as wisdom itself, presenting reverent fear as the defining orientation of the inner life that constitutes true understanding of reality.
Terrors are turned directly upon Job, consuming his honor and prosperity, showing terror as a relentless inner assault that strips identity and dignity along with outward goods.
Job's terror of divine calamity was so great he could not face God's majesty, showing dread of God as an inner restraint that shaped his moral behavior throughout his life.
Fear of public contempt so dismayed Job that he kept silent and stayed indoors, showing that social dread can paralyze the will and suppress righteous speech and action.
Job admits he stood in great trembling fear of the multitude, revealing that social pressure created an inner terror of people's opinion that could override courageous action.
Elihu was afraid to speak his opinion because of the elders' age, locating fear in the inner faculty of self-assessment that made deference feel necessary before experience.
Job's friends are dismayed into complete speechlessness, showing that being dismayed can entirely shut down the rational and verbal faculties, leaving the inner person wordless.
Elihu assures Job that his own presence should cause no terror, distinguishing himself from God and promising the freedom to speak without the crushing inner weight of divine dread.
God allures Job out of inner constraint into a broad, uncramped place, presenting constraint as an oppressive inward narrowness that God's deliverance replaces with spaciousness.
Elihu's heart trembles and leaps at the thunder of God's voice, locating trembling in the heart as the inner organ that responds with physical agitation to overwhelming divine power.
God is clothed with awesome majesty that inspires reverent fear, presenting divine splendor as the objective source that produces awe in those who perceive it.
Men fear God because of his awesome majesty, and he disregards the self-wise, showing that genuine reverent fear arises from recognizing divine greatness rather than self-exalting wisdom.
The ostrich has no fear toward her young, showing dread's absence as a structural defect in parental instinct—the inner concern that normally protects offspring is simply missing.
The war horse's majestic snorting produces terror in others, showing terror as an outward-radiating force generated by the horse's formidable presence that strikes those who encounter it.
The war horse laughs at fear and is not dismayed even by the sword, depicting courage as the complete inner absence of the dismay that would normally cause retreat.
The war horse laughs at dread and does not turn back, presenting dread's absence as a characteristic of the horse's fearless inner disposition before the terrors of battle.
Terror surrounds Leviathan's teeth like a barrier, locating terror as a quality that radiates outward from the beast's very face, overwhelming any who approach.
When Leviathan rises up, even the mighty are filled with dread and lose their senses, showing that his presence triggers an uncontrollable inner panic that overwhelms the strongest.
Leviathan is described as a creature utterly without terror — he uniquely lacks the inner experience of dread that all other earthly beings possess, marking him as incomparable in fearlessness.
God's wrathful speech produces dismay in the kings who rebel against him — the inner disruption is caused by divine fury directed against those who defy his authority.
Fear of the Lord is the proper inner orientation for those who serve him — it coexists with joy, shaping worship as a reverent, trembling delight rather than casual confidence.
Trembling accompanies rejoicing in service to the Lord — the inner state is not terror but a reverent awe that qualifies and tempers the joy of those who approach God.
The psalmist refuses to let fear take hold inwardly even when surrounded by tens of thousands of enemies — trust in God silences the inner impulse toward dread of human threat.
Trembling or agitation is channeled inward — the call is to be stirred in the heart but not to act out in sin, with the inner turbulence resolved through silent reflection on one's bed.
Fear of God is the inner disposition that draws the psalmist into worship — reverence for who God is motivates his approach to the temple and shapes his posture of bowing down.
The psalmist's bones are deeply troubled or dismayed — the inner anguish has a physical dimension, with distress penetrating to the body as an expression of profound inner disturbance.
The psalmist's soul itself is greatly dismayed — the inner being, not just the body, is the seat of this profound turmoil, and it drives the anguished cry to God asking how long.
The dismay the psalmist experienced from enemies is reversed — they themselves will be suddenly and greatly dismayed, their inner composure shattered as they turn back in shame.
The oppressor's ability to strike terror into others is the target of God's justice — when justice is done, the earthly man will no longer be able to impose dread on the vulnerable.
The wicked are seized by great dread precisely where God is present among the righteous — the inner panic is triggered by encountering the reality of God's alignment with his people.
The dread that overtakes the wicked is great and sudden — it is caused by God's presence with the righteous, exposing the inner terror that godlessness cannot escape when faced with divine reality.
Those who fear the Lord are the ones a righteous person honors — the inner disposition of reverence toward God is the mark by which worthy people are identified and esteemed.
The fear of the Lord is described as clean and enduring — unlike transient human anxiety, this reverential inner posture is morally pure and permanent, aligned with God's righteous order.
Standing in awe of God is the appropriate inner response called forth from Israel — this reverential dread accompanies praise and glorifying God, framing worship as an act of awed submission.
Those who fear the Lord are called to praise him — fear here is the inner orientation of reverence that defines the community of worshipers who belong to him.
Those who fear God are the community before whom the psalmist fulfills his vows — their shared inner orientation of reverence makes them the fitting witnesses of covenant faithfulness.
Even in the valley of death's shadow, the psalmist refuses to fear evil — divine presence displaces inner dread completely, with God's rod and staff providing the security that prevents fear.
The man who fears the Lord receives divine instruction — inner reverence toward God is the qualifying posture that opens a person to being guided in the way he should choose.
The fear of God positions a person for intimate covenant relationship with the Lord — those whose inner life is marked by this reverence receive God's friendship and knowledge of his covenant.
The psalmist's confidence in God as light and salvation eliminates inner fear — the rhetorical question 'whom shall I fear?' expresses that divine protection leaves no one and nothing left to dread.
With the Lord as the stronghold of his life, the psalmist asks of whom he should be in dread — God's protective presence makes inner terror toward any human threat irrational and unnecessary.

Even when war surrounds him, the psalmist's heart will not fear — the inner locus of fear is explicitly named as the heart, which remains confident rather than yielding to the dread that circumstances could produce.
When God hid his face, the psalmist was dismayed — the withdrawal of God's favorable presence directly produced inner collapse and disturbance, revealing how dependent the soul is on divine nearness.
The psalmist has become an object of dread to his acquaintances — his suffering causes those who know him to recoil in alarm, so that dread operates as a social and relational rupture.
The psalmist hears whispering enemies plotting against his life, and terror surrounds him on every side — this terror is the inner experience of being hunted, enclosed by threat from all directions.
God has stored up abundant goodness for those who fear him — inner reverence toward God is the condition that positions a person to receive divine provision and refuge in public.
All inhabitants of the world are called to stand in awe of God — this reverential dread is the universal inner response owed to God by all people, grounded in his sovereign creative power.
The whole earth is commanded to fear the Lord — this reverential inner response is due to God as the Most High, and it is the fitting posture of all creation before its sovereign ruler.
The Lord's watchful eye is fixed on those who fear him — inner reverence toward God draws divine attention and care, linking the inner posture of fear to the receipt of God's saving oversight.
The psalmist sought the Lord and was delivered from all his fears — these fears were the inner terrors and anxieties that had gripped him, and divine response removed them entirely.
Those who fear God receive angelic protection — inner reverence toward God is what places a person within the circle of divine deliverance, making fear of God the ground of safety.
The saints are commanded to fear the Lord with the promise that those who do will lack nothing — reverential inner fear of God is both a duty and the path to full provision and security.
Those who fear the Lord have no lack — the inner posture of reverent fear is linked to material and spiritual sufficiency, contrasting the security of the God-fearer with the want of the godless.

The fear of the Lord is something that can be taught — it is a learnable inner disposition that the psalmist offers to instruct children in, treating it as a formative orientation of the whole person.
The wicked have no dread of God before their eyes — the absence of reverential fear in their inner vision reveals a fundamental moral blindness that allows transgression to speak freely in the heart.

Many who witness God's saving work will fear and trust in him — seeing God act produces reverential awe as the inner response that then issues in faith, linking observation to transformation of the will.
God's waves and breakers have gone over the psalmist — these overwhelming forces are experienced as instruments of divine action that produce an inner sense of being engulfed and overwhelmed by God.
The king's right hand teaches awesome deeds — the awe-inspiring acts performed in battle express a fearsome power that shapes how others perceive and respond to his majesty and might.

Even if the earth gives way and mountains plunge into the sea, the community will not fear — the inner resolve against terror is grounded not in circumstances but in God's presence as refuge and strength.
The Lord Most High is to be feared as a great king over all the earth — his universal sovereignty makes reverential fear the appropriate inner response of all peoples toward him.
Seeing the city of God, the enemy kings were seized with panic and fled — the sight of God's dwelling produced sudden inner dismay so overwhelming that it drove them to immediate flight.
The kings were astounded the moment they saw the city — the inner state of shock and astonishment struck them instantly upon sight, overwhelming composure and triggering panic and flight.
Trembling seized the kings there, like the anguish of a woman in labor — the inner state is involuntary and overwhelming, a bodily-felt terror that grips and immobilizes like birth pangs.

The psalmist questions why he should fear in times of trouble when surrounded by deceitful enemies — the inner impulse to fear is challenged and reasoned against, as if fear would be an irrational concession to threat.

The psalmist commands against being afraid when the wicked prosper — the inner response of fear toward human wealth and power is presented as unwarranted given its transient and ultimately powerless nature.

The righteous will see the downfall of the arrogant and will fear — witnessing God's judgment produces reverential awe in the inner being of those aligned with God, combining fear with laughter at the fool's end.

The wicked are in great dread where there is no real cause for it — their inner terror is irrational and self-produced, a consequence of God's rejection that makes them panic even where no danger exists.
Great dread seizes those who oppose God's people even where no genuine threat exists — their inner terror is shown to be groundless panic caused by divine rejection, not by actual danger.
The pressure comes from enemy oppression — the wicked drop trouble on the psalmist and bear anger against him, producing an inner sense of being crushed by hostile, relentless external force.
Terror is located in the heart — the terrors of death fall upon the psalmist, causing inner anguish so intense it is felt as a physical collapse within the chest.
Fear comes upon the psalmist as an invading force alongside trembling and horror, overwhelming him from without and flooding through the whole inner person.
Shuddering arrives together with fear and trembling as one element of a cluster of visceral responses that overwhelm the psalmist, indicating a full-body inner collapse before threat.
Trembling comes upon the psalmist paired with fear, registering terror as a physical response that overtakes the person when horror overwhelms.
The absence of fearing God is linked to moral rigidity and refusal to change — not fearing God here marks an inner disposition of defiant self-sufficiency that invites divine judgment.
Fear is acknowledged as a real inner state, but trust in God is the willed response to it — the verse shows fear and trust coexisting, with trust as the governing act of the will.
Trust in God is the basis on which fear is overcome — the rhetorical question 'what can flesh do to me?' shows that trusting God's word strips human threat of its power to produce fear.
Trust in God is declared as the ground that removes fear of what humans can do — the inner posture of trust displaces the inner state of fear, leaving the person unshaken by mortal threat.
Those who fear God are given a banner as a place of refuge from danger — their fearing is the relational disposition that secures divine protection in the face of threat.
Fearing God's name is the defining characteristic of those who inherit what God gives — it marks an inner reverence that constitutes belonging to the community of the faithful.
Dread of the enemy is an inner threat to the psalmist's life — he asks God to preserve him from it, showing that dread itself is experienced as a life-endangering inward pressure.
The enemies shoot suddenly and 'without fear,' indicating that the absence of fear here is a moral failure — they act against the innocent with no internal restraint or reverent hesitation.
All mankind fears in response to God's public act of judgment — the fear is a universal inner reaction to witnessing divine power at work, prompting reflection and proclamation.
God answers with 'awesome deeds,' implying that his acts produce a reverential fear — the whole earth and its farthest peoples are the object of this fear-evoking salvation.
Those dwelling at the earth's ends are filled with awe at God's signs — the fear here is an inner astonishment produced by witnessing cosmic phenomena under divine control.
God's deeds are so awesome that enemies cringe before his power — the fear is an involuntary submissive response evoked by the sheer scale of divine might.
God is described as awesome in his deeds toward humanity — his acts generate a reverential fear that the whole congregation is invited to witness and confirm.
Those who fear God are identified as a community whose shared reverence qualifies them to hear testimony about what God has done — fearing God is both their identity and their orientation.
God's blessing of his people is intended to draw all the earth's peoples into reverent fear of him — fear is the fitting inner response to experienced divine blessing.
God is declared awesome from his sanctuary — the awe-inspiring quality of God is rooted in his powerful, strength-giving presence, which evokes reverent fear in those who know him.
The people are urged to fear God enduringly through all generations — fear is envisioned as an ongoing, multigenerational inner orientation rather than a momentary reaction.
God alone is to be feared, and his anger makes it impossible for anyone to stand before him — the rhetorical question drives home that fear of God is the only rational inner response to his wrath.
When God spoke judgment from heaven, the earth feared and fell silent — the trembling stillness of the earth images an inner state of absolute submission before divine authority.
God is described as one who is to be feared — all those around him are to bring gifts in recognition of this, making fear the inner disposition that motivates worship and tribute.
God is the one 'to be feared,' and all who surround him are to bring gifts — the verse presents this reverential fear as both an inner acknowledgment of his power and an external act of homage.
God is to be feared by earthly kings because he cuts off the spirit of princes — the fear is grounded in his sovereign power over human rulers, leaving no earthly power exempt.
The deep trembles when it sees God — the fear is attributed to the cosmic waters themselves, portraying an instinctive inner terror in creation's response to divine presence.
God led Israel so safely that they did not experience dread — the absence of dread is the inner evidence of God's protecting presence, contrasted with the drowning of their enemies.
The psalmist asks God to terrify his enemies with storm and hurricane — the dismay sought is an inner collapse of the enemy's confidence, overwhelmed by the experience of divine power.
The enemies are to be permanently shamed and dismayed, then perish in disgrace — the lasting dismay is an inner undoing of their self-assurance, a judgment that seals their defeat.
God's salvation is directed toward those who fear him — their fear is the relational posture that aligns them with God's saving purposes and draws his glory near.
The psalmist asks God to unite his heart in order to fear his name — fear of God requires inner integration; a divided heart is an obstacle to genuine reverence.
God's wrath lies heavy on the psalmist and his waves overwhelm — the waves are instruments of divine pressure that crush the inner person, felt as unbearable divine hostility.
The psalmist has suffered God's terrors from youth, leaving him helpless — the terror is a long-endured inner affliction attributed directly to God, producing a state of utter desolation.
God is greatly to be feared among the holy ones in the divine council — his awesomeness surpasses all heavenly beings, making reverential fear the only fitting inner response even in heaven.
God is to be feared or held in awe above all who surround him in the divine council — this trembling before God reflects the appropriate inner posture of all heavenly beings in his presence.
God's wrath brings humanity to an end, and the people are dismayed by it — the dismay is an inner dissolution caused by direct encounter with divine anger, leaving no ground to stand on.
