The verse asks who truly grasps God's anger in proportion to the fear he deserves — fear of God is the appropriate inward measure by which his wrath is rightly understood and taken seriously.

The one sheltered by God will not fear the night's terror — fear is negated by divine protection, showing that trust in God removes the inner response that darkness and danger would otherwise provoke.
The terror of the night is something the protected person will not need to fear — dread of what lurks in darkness is the specific inner threat that divine shelter renders powerless.
Anxious cares multiply within the heart, but God's consolations restore the soul — the anxiety is located in the heart as a crowding inner burden that divine comfort directly counters.
The Lord is to be feared above all gods because of his greatness — his incomparable status makes reverential fear the only fitting inner response, distinguishing him from all rivals.
The Lord's reign calls the peoples to tremble — the trembling is the inner and physical response appropriate to encountering the enthroned sovereign God.
God's great and awesome name is to be praised — the awesomeness that evokes fear is the very quality that makes his name worthy of universal praise and recognition of his holiness.
Nations and kings will fear the Lord's name and glory — the fear is a universal inner acknowledgment of divine majesty prompted by witnessing God's powerful acts on behalf of Zion.
God's steadfast love is as immeasurable as the height of heaven, directed specifically toward those who fear him — fearing God is the relational orientation that receives his boundless covenant love.
God shows fatherly compassion to those who fear him — fear here is the relational posture of a child before a father, the inner orientation that opens the person to divine tenderness.
God's everlasting steadfast love rests upon those who fear him across generations — fearing God is the covenant stance that secures the transmission of divine faithfulness to one's descendants.
When God hides his face, creatures are dismayed and die — the dismay is an existential inner collapse triggered by the withdrawal of divine presence, leading immediately to dissolution.
Egypt's dread of Israel was so overwhelming it produced gladness at their departure — the inner terror compelled relief, showing dread as a force that drives the will toward avoidance.
God's deeds at the Red Sea are called 'awesome,' pointing to a quality that evokes reverent fear in those who witness them — the term describes the awe-inspiring character of divine acts.
Coercive oppression externally crushes people, diminishing and lowering them — the inner experience is one of being pressed down and constrained by forces outside the self.
Fearing God is the inner orientation that qualifies a person to receive his provision and covenant faithfulness — it is a reverent disposition that places one within the sphere of God's care.
God's name is 'awesome,' evoking a reverential fear in those who encounter it — the term points to the inner response of awe that divine holiness properly produces.
Fear of the Lord is the foundational inner disposition from which wisdom begins — it is not peripheral but constitutive of understanding, orienting the whole mind toward God.
Fearing the Lord is paired with delighting in his commandments, showing it is an inner disposition of reverence and devotion that produces blessed obedience.
The God-fearer's heart is firm and trusting, so he is not afraid of bad news — the absence of anxious fear flows from a settled inner confidence in the Lord.
A steady heart replaces fear of adversaries — the inner being is stabilized by trust, so the will to fear is overridden until vindication comes.
Those who fear the Lord are called to trust him as their help and shield — fear of God is the inner stance that grounds reliance on him rather than on human strength.
God blesses all who fear him regardless of social standing — reverential fear of God is the one inner quality that positions a person, great or small, to receive divine blessing.
The psalmist was hemmed in by mortal anguish — distress gripped the inner person as snares and pangs closed in, producing suffering that drove him to call out for help.
Those who fear the Lord are called to testify to his enduring steadfast love — their inner reverence becomes the source of public proclamation of God's faithfulness.
Distress pressed so tightly on the psalmist that it drove him to cry out — the inner anguish served as the catalyst for prayer, and God's answer brought freedom from that constriction.
Because the Lord is on the psalmist's side, fear of human threat has no hold — the inner refusal to fear rests on the structural opposite: divine presence nullifies human threat.
The psalmist asks God to confirm his promise so that God might be feared — fear of God is the goal of divine faithfulness, the inner response God's word is meant to produce.
The psalmist dreads reproach as something he urgently wants turned away — the inner dread here is a painful anticipatory fear of public shame, directed toward a specific social threat.
The psalmist's inner reverence for God shapes his social identity — he actively seeks companionship with others who share that fear, showing it as a bond that forms community.
Those who fear God will rejoice when they see the psalmist's hope fulfilled — their shared inner reverence creates a bond of solidarity and mutual encouragement in God's word.
The psalmist desires the fellowship of those who fear God so he may share God's testimonies with them — reverent fear is the inner qualification that makes one a suitable teacher and companion.
Fear of God's judgments produces a somatic response — the psalmist's flesh trembles, showing that reverential dread penetrates from the inner person outward to the body.
Dread of God's judgments is the specific inner content — the trembling of flesh is the bodily manifestation of an awe-filled terror before God's judicial authority.
Despite princes' persecution, the psalmist's heart stands in awe of God's word — dread here is a reverent inner posture toward Scripture that overrides fear of human oppressors.
Fearing the Lord and walking in his ways are paired as the inner disposition and its outward expression — reverent fear shapes the whole life toward blessing.
Fearing the Lord is the single inner characteristic that makes a man a candidate for the described blessing — it is the defining quality of the one God blesses.
God's forgiveness is the basis for being feared — the inner response of reverent fear is properly evoked not by terror alone but by the experience of divine grace and pardon.
Those who fear the Lord are grouped with the house of Levi as worshippers called to bless God — reverential fear is the inner disposition that qualifies one for worship.
The psalmist praises God with a sense of awe at his own fearfully wonderful creation — the inner soul recognizes the astonishing nature of God's work, producing reverent gratitude.

The psalmist invites God to search his heart and know his anxious thoughts — the inner seat of anxiety is specifically the heart, and the term points to troubled, divided thoughts needing divine examination.
God's deeds are described as awesome, producing a fear-inducing wonder in those who speak of them — the term captures the inner impact of encountering divine power expressed in mighty acts.
God fulfills the desire of those who fear him and answers their cry — reverential fear is the inner relational posture that positions a person to experience God's responsive care.
God takes pleasure in those who fear him and hope in his love — the two qualities paired show that fear of God is an inner orientation of reverence and dependent trust.
Fear of the Lord is the foundational inner disposition from which all knowledge begins — without it the mind is disoriented, as fools who despise wisdom demonstrate by contrast.
Terror is depicted as striking suddenly like a storm upon those who rejected wisdom — the inner experience is one of overwhelming dread that wisdom's voice laughs at because it was foreseeable.
Dread strikes like a storm — the inner person is overwhelmed suddenly and without recourse, caught in the full force of terror that accompanies the collapse of the wicked.
Rejecting the fear of the Lord is an act of the will — the inner choice to hate knowledge and refuse reverence for God is the root cause of the disaster that follows.
The one who listens to wisdom lives without dread of disaster — the inner state of freedom from dread is the positive fruit of heeding wisdom, showing dread as wisdom's opposite.
Fear of the Lord is the fruit of earnest seeking after wisdom — it is an inner understanding arrived at through diligent pursuit, not merely an emotion but a formed insight.
Fearing the Lord is paired with turning from evil and set against self-reliant pride — it is an inner disposition of humility and reverence that reorients the whole moral life away from evil.
The one who follows wisdom need not dread while lying down — freedom from nocturnal fear is promised as an inner rest, showing dread as something wisdom removes from the sleeping mind.
The wise person is commanded not to be afraid of sudden terror — the inner disposition of trust in God is the structural opposite that displaces fearful dread of the wicked's ruin.
Sudden terror is the specific inner threat the wise person need not fear — its arrival is compared to a storm, emphasizing the overwhelming, uncontrollable nature of the dread it brings.
Fear of the Lord is defined here by its moral content — it is the inner hatred of evil, pride, and perverse speech, showing reverence for God as inseparable from moral aversion.
Fear of the Lord is the starting point of wisdom — it is the inner orientation that opens the mind to genuine understanding, without which knowledge of the Holy One cannot be attained.
What the wicked dreads will actually come upon him — his inner fear anticipates his own destruction, and ironically that feared outcome becomes his reality while the righteous receive their desire.
Fear of the Lord extends life — the inner reverential posture toward God has a direct life-shaping consequence, contrasted with the shortened existence of the wicked.
Anxiety weighs down a man's heart — the inner seat is specifically the heart, and anxiety acts as a burden pressing down the inner person, relieved only by an encouraging word.

Revering God's commandment is the inner disposition that leads to reward, contrasted with despising the word — reverential fear here is expressed as an attentive, obedient relationship to divine instruction.
Fearing the Lord is expressed in upright conduct, while devious ways reveal contempt for him — the inner disposition of reverence or contempt is evidenced by the path one walks.
The wise person's reverential caution causes him to turn from evil — the inner quality here is a wary, fear-informed prudence that restrains reckless action, contrasted with the fool's carelessness.
Fear of the Lord functions as an inner foundation of strong confidence, not anxiety, and its protective effect extends outward to one's children as a refuge.
Fear of the Lord acts as a life-giving inner orientation that actively redirects the person away from the deadly traps that would otherwise ensnare them.
Fear of the Lord is a disposition whose inner worth surpasses material wealth; its presence makes poverty preferable to riches accompanied by trouble.
Fear of the Lord is the very medium in which wisdom's instruction is received; it shapes the inner person toward teachability, and humility naturally precedes honor.
Fear of the Lord is an operative inner force that turns the person away from evil, functioning as the moral agent that drives ethical avoidance.

The verse describes self-destructive ruin without using the term's direct inner-being content; terror here is implied as outcome—the fool's own speech ensnares his soul, making his mouth the instrument of his undoing.
Fear of the Lord leads to life and produces a restful inner satisfaction; the one who has it is not visited by harm, showing it as a protective, stabilizing disposition.
The king's terror is an externally projected, lion-like force that provokes dread in subjects; provoking it costs one's life, showing it operates as coercive threat against the will.
Justice done is experienced as crushing terror by evildoers—their inner response to righteous order is dread, contrasted with the joy it brings to the righteous.
Fear of the Lord, paired with humility, is an inner disposition whose reward is concrete blessing—riches, honor, and life—confirming it as a generative, orienting virtue.
Fear of the Lord is an ongoing heart-disposition set in deliberate contrast to envy of sinners; the will is directed to continue in it all day, making it a sustained inner choice.
Fearing the Lord and the king is a commanded inner orientation of reverence toward authority; it is contrasted with joining those who do otherwise, showing it governs allegiance and conduct.
Fearing the Lord always is the inner posture that produces blessedness, set against hardening the heart; the contrast shows that this dread-reverence keeps the will pliable and safe from calamity.
The stingy man hastens after wealth in inner agitation, unaware that poverty is coming; the term points to impulsive, rash inner drive rather than outward terror in this verse.
Fear of man is an inner trap—a trembling anxiety before people that ensnares the person—while trusting the Lord provides the inner safety that replaces it.

The term 'coercion' (o.tser) here describes the barren womb's relentless inner constraint of unfulfilled longing; it is listed among things never satisfied, suggesting anguished compulsion without relief.
The virtuous woman is not afraid of snow's threat to her household because she has prepared adequately; the fear that does not arise here is practical dread for her dependents' welfare.
A woman who fears the Lord is contrasted with the deceit of charm and the vanity of beauty; her reverent fear is the inner quality that makes her truly praiseworthy.
God's eternal, unalterable works are designed so that people fear before him—a reverential awe that is a fitting inner response to divine sovereignty over all things.
Rashness of mouth and hastiness of heart before God are warned against; the inner impulse to speak quickly is the very error, contrasted with the few, measured words proper before the divine.
Amid the vanity of many dreams and words, fearing God is the one essential inner stance required; it stands as the corrective to empty, unfocused inner activity.

The observer is told not to be amazed at injustice and oppression in the province; the inner response of astonishment is discouraged because a higher hierarchy of oversight exists above every official.
Being quick in spirit to anger is the inner rashness warned against; anger that lodges in the heart of fools is a settled inner state, not a passing reaction.
The God-fearer is commended for navigating both extremes without falling into either; fearing God is the inner governing disposition that enables balanced, wise conduct.
Hastiness to leave the king's presence is warned against; the inner impulse of rash urgency before power is the error, since the king acts as he pleases and rashness invites danger.
Those who fear God—maintaining reverent orientation toward him—will fare well, even as the sinner prolongs life; fearing God is the inner posture that secures ultimate good.
God-fearers are those who fear before him—a reverent, submissive inner stance—and this is precisely why it will be well with them, distinguishing them from the wicked.
The wicked man does not fear before God—his lack of inner reverence is the defining characteristic that explains why it will not be well with him, even if he lives long.

The God-fearer is listed among those who share the same fate as all others; the term here identifies a recognized inner-life category, but the verse's point is that external outcomes do not distinguish him.
Terrors are in the way for the aging person approaching death; the inner experience is one of dread at life's journey as desire fails and the eternal home nears.
The aging person is afraid of what is high—a fear of heights or elevated dangers—alongside road-terrors; both reflect a fragile, diminished inner sense of security in the face of approaching death.
Fearing God, paired with keeping his commandments, is declared the whole duty of man—the comprehensive inner orientation from which all human obligation flows.
Armed soldiers guard against terror by night; the dread here is an external nocturnal threat to be defended against, showing fear as a danger confronting the beloved's procession.
The beloved is described as awesome as an army with banners; this terrible, overwhelming quality projects an awe-inducing power that provokes a kind of reverent dread in the beholder.
The woman's beauty is again compared to an awe-inspiring army; the term marks an overwhelming, almost fearful grandeur that exceeds ordinary beauty and commands a kind of inner overwhelm.
The whole heart is faint—an exhausted, weakened inner condition caused by repeated rebellion and divine chastisement, leaving the inner person depleted and without strength.
People are commanded to hide in rock and dust from the Lord's terror—a dread so overwhelming it drives the will to seek physical concealment from his majestic presence.
When the Lord rises to terrify the earth, people flee into caves; the trembling here is the bodily and inner convulsion produced by divine majesty that overwhelms human composure.
The Lord's dread drives people to hide in the earth's caverns; the terror is his majestic rising in judgment, compelling flight as the only inner and bodily response.
The Lord's rising to terrify the earth again drives people into rock-clefts; the trembling he produces is a response to his splendor and majesty that overwhelms all human composure.
People hide in cliff-clefts from the Lord's dread and splendor as he rises in judgment; the dread is his manifested glory that the inner person cannot endure and must flee.
The fierce burning anger of Rezin and the king of Syria is the external threat; Ahaz is told not to let his heart be faint because of it, showing the inner impact of threatening rage on the will.
Ahaz is commanded not to fear the two hostile kings despite their menace; the command targets his inner disposition, calling him to quiet the heart rather than be driven by dread.
Isaiah is commanded not to adopt the people's conspiratorial fear—not to fear what they fear; the inner disposition of communal dread is to be rejected in favor of a different object of fear.
The people's fear—what they are afraid of—is explicitly named as the wrong object; the verse warns the prophet away from internalizing the popular dread that drives the crowd.
The same people's fear (mo.ra) that the crowd holds is again what the prophet is told to reject; this fear-object is a socially shared inner dread that distorts perception of true danger.
The command 'do not be in dread' closes the warning; the prophet is to resist the trembling-dread that the crowd feels, refusing to let it govern his inner life or action.
The Lord of hosts is to be the sole object of the prophet's fear; this redirects the inner emotion of dread from political threats to the holy God who alone merits such reverent awe.
The Lord is to be 'your fear'—the personal inner object of reverent dread—set against the crowd's fear of human powers; it is a deliberate reorientation of what the heart holds in awe.
The Lord is to be the one before whom the prophet trembles in dread; this redirects the inner impulse of trembling-fear away from conspiracies and human threats toward the holy God alone.
The inner state of anguish—tight, crushing pressure on the person—is contrasted with the coming relief; constraint names the oppressive inner distress that the promised glory will end.
God's people dwelling in Zion are commanded not to let fear take hold inwardly when the Assyrians threaten with force; the command targets the inner disposition before an intimidating enemy.
Ramah trembles as the Assyrian army approaches—the town's population is gripped by visceral, involuntary shaking produced by the nearness of overwhelming military threat.
The Lord's terrifying power is the force by which he will cut down the proud and mighty; terror here describes the overwhelming, awe-striking quality of divine judgment that brings the lofty low.
Fear of the Lord is listed as a Spirit-given inner quality resting upon the coming messianic king, placing it alongside wisdom, understanding, counsel and might as a constitutive inner disposition.
Fear of the Lord is the king's inner delight—not a burden but a willing, heartfelt orientation—and it governs his judgment independently of surface appearances or hearsay.
Trust in God as salvation displaces dread from the inner person; the speaker declares that because God is his strength, fear no longer has a hold on his heart.
Dismay seizes the inner person with a force like birth pangs—overwhelming, uncontrollable distress that leaves people staring helplessly at one another in shared horror.
People look at one another in stunned, wide-eyed astonishment as disaster overwhelms them; the inner faculty of comprehension is undone—they can only gaze in shock.
Sheol itself is stirred into agitated commotion at the descent of the fallen king—the realm of the dead is aroused and unsettled, a kind of cosmic trembling at his arrival.
Onlookers marvel that this once-fearsome king made the whole earth tremble—the trembling describes the terror-induced shaking he formerly imposed on the inner composure of nations and kingdoms.
Terror arrives suddenly at evening and is gone by morning, describing a sudden overwhelming dread that strikes the enemies of God's people—intense, acute, and utterly annihilating.
The nation described is one that inspires fear in others near and far—their power produces a pervasive, wide-ranging dread in those who encounter or hear of them.
The same people who inspire widespread fear in others will ultimately bring tribute to the Lord; their fearsome reputation is acknowledged even as they are directed toward God's sovereignty.
Egypt's inner state collapses to the weakness of women trembling—their trembling is produced specifically by seeing the Lord's raised hand, a fear directed at divine power acting against them.
The Egyptians tremble with dread before the Lord's shaking hand; the dread is inwardly directed—it unmans them, reducing them to helpless fear before God's threatening action.
Judah becomes an object of terror to Egypt—merely mentioning it provokes fear in Egyptians because of what God has purposed against them; the terror is caused by divine intent, not Judah's own might.
Every Egyptian who hears the name of Judah dreads it because they sense the Lord's purposeful judgment behind it; the inner dread is rationally grounded in awareness of divine hostility.
Those who had placed their hope in Cush and Egypt are dismayed and ashamed when those powers fail them—their inner collapse comes from misplaced confidence being shattered.

Dismay so overwhelms the prophet's inner faculties that he cannot see—the characteristic has shut down normal perception, leaving him functionally incapacitated by what he has witnessed in the vision.
Trembling replaces the twilight the prophet had longed for—anticipated rest is converted into involuntary inner shaking as horror seizes the heart, turning desire into dread.
Horror shudders through the prophet's heart, appalling him; the shuddering is located explicitly in the heart, the center of inner life, as the dreadful vision strikes to the core.
Dread is listed alongside pit and snare as inescapable dangers closing in on earth's inhabitants—it names an overwhelming, inescapable inner fear that accompanies divine judgment.
Fleeing at the sound of the dread drives a person into the pit—the inner terror triggers flight but flight only leads to greater disaster; dread is both symptom and trap.
Ruthless nations will fear God because of his great acts; their fear is a response of awe-reverence toward divine power, moving them to glorify rather than resist him.
Understanding the message of judgment brings sheer terror—grasping its content produces a trembling horror, making comprehension itself terrifying rather than comforting.
The people are called to be astonished at themselves—a self-directed bewilderment linked to spiritual blindness and intoxication, as if stunned incomprehension has become their settled state.
The people's fear of God is exposed as hollow—a learned external formula with no inner reality, because their hearts are far from him while their lips perform reverence.
When God's people see his redemptive work among them, they will stand in awe of the God of Israel—a reverential trembling that is the inner response to recognizing divine holiness at work.
The Assyrians will be terror-stricken specifically by the Lord's voice—the inner dismay is not produced by a human army but by the direct divine word striking with his rod.
The lion is not dismayed or daunted by the shepherds' noise—used as an image of God's undaunted resolve, the verse defines the characteristic by its absence: God's will is not shaken by opposition.
Assyria's military foundation crumbles in terror and officers flee in panic—dismay causes total inner collapse and desertion, with no capacity to stand before the Lord's advancing judgment.
Assyria's rock—its stronghold or god—passes away in terror; the terror is the inner dissolution that overcomes even what seemed most solid and secure when God moves against it.
The complacent women will shudder within about a year when the harvest fails—the shuddering is a future inner shock that will break through their current sense of ease and security.
The prophet commands trembling upon complacent women as a fitting inner response to coming disaster—the call to tremble is an urgent wake-up from false security to realistic dread.
The command to tremble follows the command to strip and wear sackcloth—the inner trembling and outward mourning acts belong together as the appropriate response to the impending catastrophe.
Fear of the Lord is named Zion's treasure—it is a precious inner disposition that undergirds stability, salvation, wisdom and knowledge as the community's most valued possession.
Sinners in Zion are afraid—their dread is triggered by confronting the consuming fire of God's holiness, recognizing that their guilt makes them unable to survive his presence.
Trembling seizes the godless in Zion as they face divine fire—the trembling is a physical-inner seizure of the body and will, produced by the terrifying recognition of their own unworthiness.
The heart will muse on the past terror after the enemy is gone—the inner faculty of reflection revisits the dread once it has passed, measuring relief against what had been feared.
Those with anxious hearts are commanded 'fear not'—the command targets the inner disposition of dread directly, countered by the promise that God himself is coming to save.
Isaiah commands Hezekiah's servants not to be afraid of Assyrian blasphemy—the prohibition addresses the inner response to threatening words, assuring that God's word overrides human intimidation.
Stripped of strength, the conquered peoples are dismayed and confounded—inner collapse follows the loss of power, leaving them as fragile and helpless as blighted grass.
The herald is commanded 'fear not' as she lifts her voice to announce God's coming—the prohibition removes inner inhibition so that proclamation can be bold and unhindered.
The ends of the earth tremble as God acts—the trembling is a widespread inner response of the nations to the display of divine power, even as they move toward him.
The coastlands see God's action and are afraid—their fear is the inward recoil of those who witness divine power at work and recognize they stand before something beyond their control.
God's direct personal presence ('I am with you') is given as the reason to renounce fear—the inner disposition of dread is to be displaced by the certainty of divine accompaniment and help.
God himself holds Israel's hand and commands 'fear not'—the physical-relational image grounds the prohibition against inner fear in the certainty of divine grip and personal help.
Even weak, worm-like Jacob is told 'fear not'—the contrast between Israel's fragility and God's redemptive identity ('your Redeemer') is the basis for releasing inner dread.
God commands Jacob 'fear not' on the grounds of redemption and personal naming—the inner fear is to be surrendered because belonging to God ('you are mine') removes the ground for dread.

The inner fear is directly countered by God's personal presence ('I am with you'), which removes the ground for dread; the command 'fear not' addresses an inward disposition that God's nearness is meant to dissolve.
The inner fear is quieted by the assurance that God formed and chose Jacob from the womb; divine election and ongoing help are presented as the basis for releasing the inner anxiety of the servant.
Dread is addressed as needless because God's prior testimony and the people's own witness-role should settle the inner person; knowing there is no other God eliminates the inner ground for dread.
Being afraid is paired with 'fear not' and countered by God's long-declared word; the inner state of fright is portrayed as having no valid object once God's sole sovereignty is acknowledged.
The craftsmen who make idols will themselves be terrified and shamed; the dread they experience is an inner collapse that comes upon them when their human-made gods are exposed as powerless.
Babylon is challenged to use her sorceries to inspire terror in others; the inner capacity to produce awe-driven dread in enemies is presented as the ultimate test of her magical power.
The one who 'fears the Lord' is defined by obedience to the servant's voice; even walking in personal darkness, this inner reverence leads to trust in God rather than self-reliance.
Those whose heart holds God's law are commanded not to be inwardly dismayed by human contempt; having righteousness internalized makes the inner being resilient against revilings.
Fear of human reproach is addressed as incompatible with having God's law in the heart; the inner person whose identity rests on righteousness need not dread what mortals say.

Being afraid of mortal, grass-like men is exposed as irrational when God himself is the comforter; the inner fear of humans is challenged by redirecting the soul's attention to God's superior reality.

Continual daily dread of the oppressor's wrath is depicted as the inner experience of those who have forgotten their Maker; the dread fills the inner life precisely because the greater reality of God has been displaced.

Oppression here refers to external judicial and social coercion used to remove the servant; it does not directly describe an inner-being state but rather the external force applied to him, with no one considering the injustice done.
Fear is linked to shame and disgrace from the past; the inner disposition of dread is to be released because God promises that past humiliation will be forgotten and replaced with a new identity.
Being established in righteousness produces an inner state free from fear; oppression and terror are named as the threats that will be kept far off, so the inner person will not be gripped by them.

Terror is paired with oppression as an inward and outward threat that righteousness will keep far from the restored community; the promise that terror 'shall not come near' addresses its power to afflict the inner person.
God questions whom Israel dreaded and feared so much that she lied and forgot him; the inner fear of other powers is exposed as the root of unfaithfulness and of suppressing reverence toward God.
The nations will fear the Lord's name from east to west when he comes in power; this reverential inner awe is awakened by the display of his glory and might, not by prior moral response.
The heart thrilling and exulting is described with a word that can carry a trembling quality; here it is positive awe-struck joy in the heart at the sudden inflow of nations' wealth to restored Zion.

The people plead that God himself has hardened their heart so that they do not fear him; the absence of fear is portrayed as a deep inner failure rooted in a hardened will, not merely outward disobedience.

The nations trembling at God's presence is the desired inner-outward response to a theophany of fire and power; the trembling is produced by direct encounter with divine presence and is the proper creaturely response.

God's past awesome acts caused mountains to quake; the verse highlights that surprising divine intervention produces awe that surpasses human expectation, implying the inner impact of encountering the wholly unexpected God.

Trembling at God's word is paired with humility and a contrite spirit as the inner posture God looks for; the trembling is located in the spirit and shows reverence that orients the whole inner person toward God's communication.

God will bring upon the disobedient the very fears they harbored; the inner anxieties and dreads are treated as consequences fitting their rejection of God, turning their own fears into the instrument of judgment.

Those who tremble at God's word form a defined inner community; their trembling reverence marks their identity and sets them apart from brothers who hate them, with God's vindication promised to this inner posture.

The prophet is commanded not to be inwardly afraid of his hostile audience; God's promise of personal presence and deliverance is the direct counter to the fear that would silence the inner will to speak.

Jeremiah is warned not to let dismay at opponents grip him inwardly, with the sobering threat that if he is dismayed before them, God will dismay him before them — fear is thus a contagious inner collapse with real consequences.

The absence of the fear/dread of God is diagnosed as the inner root of Israel's apostasy; forsaking the Lord is described as evil and bitter, with the missing reverence being the inner disposition that should have held loyalty in place.

Judah did not fear despite witnessing Israel's divorce-judgment for adultery; the inner failure to be moved to reverence by observed consequences shows a will hardened against the corrective lesson before her.

The prophets are described as astounded — an inner state of stunned incomprehension — when the expected order collapses; the astonishment registers the overwhelming shock that their assumptions about divine protection prove false.

God confronts the people's inner failure to fear him, pointing to the sea's obedient trembling before his boundary as shaming contrast; the expected inner posture is awe that recognizes God's sovereign ordering of creation.

The people do not say in their hearts 'let us fear the Lord'; the absence of reverential fear is located specifically in the heart and shown by failure to acknowledge God as the giver of seasonal rains — ingratitude and irreverence are one.

Terror surrounds every direction so that going out is unsafe; the terror is an external atmosphere that produces inner dread, hemming the person in with no safe path as the enemy's sword pervades the landscape.
The wise men are dismayed and taken because they rejected God's word; their inner collapse under judgment is the direct consequence of intellectual pride that displaced divine wisdom.
Terror arrives where healing was expected; the inner experience of shattered hope turns into shock as what was anticipated — peace and recovery — is replaced by something that dismays and overwhelms.
The heart being 'sick within' captures an inner faintness — a collapse of emotional and spiritual vitality — as grief and lost joy afflict the prophet's core being in response to his people's ruin.
Israel is commanded not to be dismayed by heavenly signs that terrify the nations; the inner susceptibility to omen-induced dread is identified as a pagan response that God's people must not adopt.

Idol-like scarecrows that cannot speak or act are declared unworthy objects of inner fear; the command not to be afraid of them is grounded in their complete impotence — they hold no power to harm or benefit.

Fear of God as King of the nations is presented as the only rational inner response, since no one among all the wise or mighty kingdoms compares to him; reverence is his due because of his incomparable greatness.
Terror replaces hoped-for healing as Judah's experience of divine judgment; the inner expectation of restoration is met with dismay when what arrives is something that shocks and overwhelms the soul.

Jeremiah pleads that God not become a terror to him, but rather his refuge; the inner dread of divine abandonment is the fear behind the prayer, with God's protective presence the only counter to that terror.

Jeremiah prays that his persecutors be dismayed while he himself is spared that inner collapse; the contrast shows dismay as a punitive inner shattering that he asks God to direct toward the guilty, not toward him.

God will make Pashhur a terror to himself and his friends; the terror is not merely external reputation but an inward self-dread, where the person becomes their own source of horror as judgment falls.

Jeremiah hears whispers of 'terror on every side' used against him by enemies watching for his fall; the terror is both an outer social threat and an inner psychological pressure that seeks to break his confidence.
Jehoiachin is handed over to the very enemies he fears; the inner object of his dread — those whose power over him he dreads — becomes the instrument of his judgment, so that fear and fate converge.
Under faithful shepherds, the flock will no longer fear or be dismayed; the inner state of security promised is the opposite of the panic caused by scatter and predation under bad leadership.
The restored flock will 'fear no more'; the inner peace promised is freedom from the ongoing dread produced by poor shepherding and exile, replaced by the security of God-appointed care.
Hezekiah feared the Lord and entreated him, and disaster was averted; the inner reverence is shown to be an active posture that turned toward God in prayer, producing a concrete historical outcome.

Uriah's fear on hearing of the king's intent to kill him drove him to flee to Egypt; the inner dread is immediately translated into decisive self-preserving action, showing fear operating through the will to escape.

Trembling is heard as part of a collective cry of panic with no peace; the sound of trembling in the community signals an inner state of collective terror that has broken through into audible distress.
Dread is voiced as a cry alongside terror with no peace to follow; the inner experience of dread is so acute it becomes audible in panic-cries, marking a community gripped by overwhelming fear.
The promise of salvation and peaceful return removes all cause for trembling; none shall make Jacob afraid, so the inner alarm that drives trembling is fully silenced by divine rescue.
Dismay is the inner collapse that God forbids Israel to experience; the assurance of salvation and quiet rest is set as the direct antidote to that inner shattering.
Fear is forbidden as a response to exile; God's promise of salvation and return is the ground on which the inner disposition of fear is to be set aside entirely.
The great terror here is the awe-inducing dread God produced in Egypt's inhabitants through signs and wonders, functioning as an instrument of divine power felt by those who opposed Israel's exodus.
Fear here is the overwhelming dread of God displayed outwardly in the Exodus through mighty acts, producing terror in those who witnessed or experienced his power against Egypt.
Reverential fear of God is an inner orientation God promises to produce in his people through a unified heart and way, directed perpetually toward him for their own lasting good.
God pledges to implant the fear of himself directly into the hearts of his people so that it becomes a permanent inner disposition preventing them from turning away from him.
The nations' inner response to hearing of Jerusalem's restoration is dread and trembling — the good God does for the city produces awe that shakes them to their core.
Trembling accompanies dread as the nations' involuntary inner and physical response to the astonishing good and prosperity God pours out on Jerusalem, overwhelming their composure.
On hearing Jeremiah's words, the officials turned to one another in dread — the prophetic words produced an immediate, socially visible inner alarm that they could not suppress.
The king and his servants heard the same words yet felt no dread — the absence of afraid response reveals a hardened inner disposition unmoved by divine warning, in stark contrast to the officials.
Ebed-melech's fear is directed toward specific men whose power threatens him; God's promise of deliverance specifically addresses and overrides that inner dread of dangerous human opponents.
Fear of the Chaldeans is the inner obstacle to settling the land; Gedaliah's sworn assurance directly targets that fear, urging the people to let it go and dwell in peace.
Fear of the Chaldeans drove the people's movement toward Egypt; the assassination of Gedaliah intensified that pre-existing inner dread and made flight feel the only option.
Fear of the king of Babylon is named twice as the inner grip holding the people; God's command not to fear is grounded in his promised presence with them, directly countering that dread.
Being 'afraid' of Babylon describes the people's current inner state of object-directed dread; God's instruction to not fear him challenges them to abandon that settled disposition.
The sword they fear will pursue and overtake them in Egypt — the object of their fear will become inescapable, so the inner dread proves ultimately futile as a guide for their choices.
The failure to fear God is paired with refusing to humble themselves and walk in his law — the absence of reverential fear is an inner posture of rebellion that explains their continued disobedience.
Terror on every side is the battle-induced inner collapse that causes Egypt's warriors to abandon their posts and flee, their inner resolve shattered by overwhelming dread.
God commands Jacob not to tremble, promising salvation and peaceful return; the trembling that would naturally arise from captivity is to be stilled by trust in divine deliverance.
Dismay is forbidden to Israel in the face of captivity; the divine promise of return and rest is the inner basis on which that inner shattering should not take hold.
Fear is commanded away from Jacob as God promises to save and restore; the inner disposition of fear is incompatible with the rest and ease that divine rescue will produce.
God's presence with Jacob is the direct ground for the command not to fear; the inner disposition of fear is to yield to assured divine companionship even amid just discipline.
Moab's dismay is inseparable from its being broken and shamed; the inner collapse is expressed outwardly in wailing, showing that dismay shatters both composure and national identity.
Moab's dismay is displayed in turning its back in shame; the inner collapse of a nation once proud has become a spectacle of horror that its neighbors now witness and react to.
Moab has become a horror — its ruin is so complete that it instills terror in surrounding peoples, transforming the nation into an object that produces dread in all who observe it.
Terror, pit, and snare are placed before Moab as inescapable threats; dread is the pervasive inner state of those who face divine judgment with no avenue of escape.
Fleeing from terror only delivers one into greater danger — dread is the inner driver of flight, yet God's judgment makes every escape route another trap, so fear itself leads to ruin.
God will bring terror upon Ammon from all surrounding peoples; this externally inflicted dread will drive every person to flee in isolation, scattering them without any regathering.
Edom's pride in its mountain strongholds was fed by the terror it inspired in others, but that very reputation deceived Edom into false security — the fear it caused others could not protect it.
Anxiety seizes Damascus as troubled restlessness on hearing bad news; the inner state is compared to a churning sea unable to be still, showing anxiety as an agitated, uncontrollable inner condition.
Panic seizes Damascus as it turns to flee, gripping it alongside anguish and birth-pangs; the inner experience is sudden, overwhelming, and physically incapacitating, driving flight and helplessness.
Terror on every side is the cry shouted at the nomadic peoples of Kedar; it names the encompassing dread that accompanies the total plundering of their possessions and way of life.
God will terrify Elam before their enemies, making dismay the inner condition through which he breaks their will; his fierce anger is the divine source of that shattering inner dread.
Babylon's gods Bel and Merodach are dismayed at the announcement of Babylon's fall; the inner collapse that dismay represents is applied even to idols, showing the totality of Babylon's defeat.
God's redemptive strength brings rest to the earth but unrest — agitation and trembling — to Babylon's inhabitants; their inner turmoil is the direct consequence of God championing Israel's cause.
Babylon's warriors are struck with dismay by the sword, their inner resolve destroyed; their collapse is the inner dimension of the military defeat that will bring down Babylon's defenses.
Panic seizes Babylon's soldiers as their escape routes are cut off and the marshes set ablaze; the inner state of panic accompanies the physical situation of being trapped with no way out.
Fearfulness of successive alarming reports is an inner response God warns against; the heart is told not to faint or become fearful even as violence and political upheaval fill the land.

Distress here describes the crushing inner-and-outer straits of Judah in exile — pursued without rest, finding no refuge, the word captures the anguished constriction of a people hemmed in by suffering.
The heart is faint — the inner seat of the person has lost all strength under the weight of many groans and transgressions; faintness here is the exhausted collapse of the inner person under grief.
God's fierce anger is described as burning — an intense consuming fire that expresses the inner heat of divine wrath, directed against Israel and consuming all that surrounded Jacob.
God summoned terrors on every side as if calling guests to a festival; the day of his anger became the moment when those terrors consumed all that the speaker had held and raised.
Tribulation alongside bitterness describes the experiential hardship of being besieged and enveloped by God's discipline — the inner and outer weight of suffering pressing in from all sides.
Panic and pitfall arrive together as paired experiences of judgment; dread names the internal state of alarm that precedes the external pitfall, both coming upon the community in its ruin.
When the speaker called out, God drew near and spoke 'Do not fear' — the divine response to the inner cry is direct personal reassurance, addressing fear with the intimacy of God's nearness.
The expanse above the living creatures shines like awe-inspiring crystal — the word conveys the fearsome, overwhelming quality of the vision's appearance, producing reverent dread in the beholder.

Fear of the rebellious people is forbidden to Ezekiel three times despite briers, thorns, and scorpions; the inner disposition of fear toward intimidating opposition must yield to the prophetic calling.
God gives Ezekiel a forehead harder than flint precisely so he will not fear the rebellious house; the inner disposition of fear toward opponents is overcome by a God-given inner toughness.
Anxiety accompanies eating bread by weight in besieged Jerusalem — the inner state of anxious worry governs every rationed meal, making anxiety a constant companion of scarcity and divine judgment.
Shuddering is an outward covering that overtakes the whole person—like sackcloth it wraps the body—expressing the inner horror of impending judgment, visible on every face and head.
Dismay so overwhelms the people that their hands are paralyzed—the inner terror produced by divine judgment physically disables the will and capacity for action.

The fear here is misplaced dread of the sword rather than reverence for God; Israel's inner orientation toward human threat rather than divine authority becomes the ground of their judgment—the very sword they feared is brought upon them.
Anxiety so saturates the inner person that even the basic acts of eating and drinking cannot be done without it—it is a constant, pervasive inner dread that accompanies every moment of existence.
Quivering accompanies drinking water, showing that inner agitation has become so pervasive it manifests in the body during the most ordinary acts of daily survival.
Anxiety over coming judgment shapes every meal for Jerusalem's inhabitants—inner dread of devastation attaches itself to the act of eating, signaling a life consumed by foreboding.
Here the term describes Israel's provocation of God to rage rather than personal trembling—the verse uses it for the inner fury aroused in God by Israel's unfaithfulness, not human fear.

Trembling seizes the sea-princes at Tyre's fall so completely that they clothe themselves with it—it becomes like a garment, an all-enveloping inner and bodily state produced by witnessing catastrophic destruction.

Trembling is described as a garment the sea-princes put on—it is an all-consuming inner response to Tyre's ruin that overtakes their whole being, expressed in posture, clothing, and sitting on the ground.
Terror here is Tyre's active force projected outward onto all her inhabitants—it was the city's defining power, the dread she instilled in others, now lamented as lost with her fall.
Dismay strikes the coastlands at Tyre's passing—the inner shock of witnessing an invincible city collapse generates a communal state of stunned dread across all who depended on or feared her.
Trembling seizes the coastlands on the very day of Tyre's fall—the collapse of a great power triggers an immediate, bodily-felt inner shaking in all who witness it.
Shuddering is so intense at Tyre's destruction that kings' hair bristles—the inner horror of witnessing the city's ruin produces an involuntary physical reaction that overrides composure and dignity.
Shuddering erupts physically in the kings—hair bristling with horror—showing that the inner response to Tyre's destruction overwhelms the body, producing visible, uncontrolled signs of dread.

Trembling is inflicted on unsuspecting Cushites by divine messengers—it is an externally induced inner terror that catches people off guard, overwhelming them without warning on the day of Egypt's doom.

Fear is something God actively places in the land of Egypt—it is a divinely imposed inner state, not arising from within the people but installed by judgment when their protective idols are destroyed.

Trembling is continual and self-focused—each person trembles every moment for his own life—showing that the inner experience is an ongoing, personal dread of imminent death triggered by God's brandished sword.
Shuddering manifests as kings' hair bristling when God's sword is brandished—the inner horror at divine judgment breaks through bodily composure in an involuntary, visceral reaction.

Shuddering is the involuntary inner-and-bodily response of kings to Egypt's downfall—their hair bristles with horror, showing that the sight of divine destruction bypasses rational control and seizes the whole person.
Terror is the power Assyria wielded over the living—it was the active force projected from her military might, now stripped away in death, where she lies powerless in the pit.
Terror was Elam's instrument of domination over the living—an outward force she spread by military might—but death ends it, and in Sheol she bears shame rather than inspiring dread.
Terror spread by the slain among the living is now replaced by shame in death—the verse contrasts the dread once projected outward in life with the dishonor that defines them in the pit.
Terror was Meshech-Tubal's characteristic instrument against the living—the inner dread they instilled in others defined their power, now ended in death alongside all the other fallen nations.

Terror of the mighty was their defining inner force projected over the land of the living—even in death their weapons accompany them, but the verse implies that terror is now finally silenced in Sheol.
Terror was the power the northern princes exercised through military might—it was their defining public force over the living, now exchanged for shame as they lie uncircumcised among the slain.
God himself declares he spread terror in the land of the living—framing Pharaoh's dread-inducing power as ultimately under divine control, now ended as Pharaoh is laid among the uncircumcised slain.
Trembling is defined by its absence—none shall make them afraid—indicating that the restored inner state of God's flock is security free from the dread that had previously driven them as prey.

Trembling is again defined by its absence in the restoration—dwelling securely with none to cause fear marks the healed inner condition of Israel, contrasting with the shame and dread of their prior exile.

The official's fear is directed toward the king's authority—a personal, self-preserving inner dread that the king will notice Daniel's changed condition and hold him responsible, endangering his life.

Haste here reflects urgency rather than inner dismay; the verse does not directly evidence a fear-state—Arioch moves quickly with good news, making this a marginal instance of the term's characteristic content.
The great image's appearance was frightening—its exceeding brightness and might produce a visual impression that generates fear as the inner response to an overwhelming, awe-inspiring sight.
Dismay seizes Nebuchadnezzar at the sight of a fourth figure in the fire—the inner shock of the impossible overturns his composure and drives him to his feet in haste, demanding an explanation.
Startlement seizes Nebuchadnezzar at the unexpected sight of a fourth figure in the furnace—the inner jolt of the inexplicable overrides calm and produces immediate, alarmed physical movement.
The dream's visions alarm Nebuchadnezzar while he lies in bed—fear arises from within the mind through dream-content, disturbing the inner person in the vulnerable, unguarded state of sleep.

Fear is produced by a dream that targets the mind—the visions of the head alarm the inner person, showing that dread can be generated internally through the mind's nocturnal experience without any external threat being present.

Dismay holds Daniel for a moment as his thoughts race—the inner impact of understanding a terrible message temporarily overrides speech and composure, with alarmed thoughts pressing on the inner person.

Dismay at the writing on the wall strikes Belshazzar's inner person so violently that it changes his color and causes his limbs to give way and his knees to knock—inner terror fully collapses bodily control.

Dismay greatly intensifies in Belshazzar—his color changes, showing the inner alarm has physical manifestation—and spreads to his lords as perplexity, indicating the inner disturbance is collective and unresolved.

Perplexity overtakes Belshazzar's lords alongside his alarm—their inner faculties of understanding are confounded by the mysterious writing, leaving them without explanation or resource in the face of the unknown.

The queen urges the king not to let thoughts alarm him or change his color—she recognizes that dismay originates in the inner life of thought and then visibly destabilizes the body, and she calls for inner mastery over it.
Fear before Nebuchadnezzar is the inner posture of all peoples toward his absolute power over life and death—it is coerced reverence produced by total vulnerability before an omnipotent human ruler.

The king's haste to the lion's den at daybreak reflects an anxious, urgent inner state—the term here points to the driven, agitated quality of one who has spent the night in dread and can no longer delay.

Fear before the God of Daniel is decreed as an obligatory inner posture for all peoples—it is grounded in his living, eternal, indestructible nature, making him the only object worthy of such reverential dread.

The fourth beast is described as terrifying—its appearance produces fear as the immediate inner response to its dreadful strength and destructive power, overwhelming perception with dread of what it represents.

Dismay seizes Daniel's spirit within him as the visions alarm his mind—the inner person is the explicit locus: spirit and head-visions together produce an inner disturbance that cannot be separated from the self's core.

Distress is located explicitly within Daniel's spirit—the visions grip the innermost self, producing an anxious, troubled state at the core of the person rather than merely an external or cognitive reaction.

The fourth beast is exceedingly terrifying—fear is the inner response Daniel desires to understand, implying that the beast's character is defined by the overwhelming dread it produces in those who perceive it.

Dismay works through Daniel's thoughts and changes his color—the inner agitation originates in the mind, radiates to the body, yet Daniel resolves to keep it inward, holding the matter in his heart rather than disclosing it.

Reverential fear of God is the inner disposition from which Daniel's prayer arises—God's awesome greatness and covenant faithfulness are the object, producing a posture of humble, devoted awe rather than terror.

Great trembling falls externally upon those who did not see the vision—even without perceiving its content, they are struck by an overwhelming, involuntary inner-and-bodily fear that drives them to flee and hide.

Standing in the presence of the angelic messenger, Daniel's body trembled as an involuntary physical response to the overwhelming divine encounter, expressing inner awe and weakness before heavenly reality.

The angel commands Daniel not to fear, indicating that reverential dread had gripped him from the first moment; his humble, seeking heart is contrasted with that fear, showing fear is the natural inner response to divine presence.

Fear is commanded away and replaced by peace and strength; the inner person moves from fearful weakness to fortified courage as the heavenly messenger speaks, showing that fear yields to strength when God's word comes.
Alarming news from the east and north strikes the king's inner being with dismay, driving him outward in furious, destructive rage—fear here converts inward alarm into violent external action.

Israel's returning to God is described as coming 'in fear' to him, depicting reverential dread mixed with awe as the inner posture of restored relationship—fear directed toward the Lord and his goodness.

Israel's failure to fear the Lord is cited as the root cause of their loss of kingship; the absence of reverential fear in the inner will has left them without divine authorization and political power.

The inhabitants of Samaria tremble with anxious dread over their idol calf, showing that misplaced fear has attached itself to a false object—the inner emotion of dread is real but directed toward what cannot save.
At God's lion-like roar, his scattered children come trembling from the west—the trembling is an awe-driven, compelled response to divine power that draws them back home in fearful obedience.
Like startled birds, the exiles come trembling from Egypt and Assyria, depicting the inner state of awed, anxious urgency that accompanies their divinely prompted return home.

When Ephraim spoke, trembling was the response of others, indicating a former authoritative presence that inspired awe; this inner-commanding stature was then lost through idolatry and spiritual death.
The trumpet alarm calls all inhabitants to tremble before the approaching day of the Lord, making communal terror an appropriate and expected inner response to imminent divine judgment.
The awesome greatness of the Lord's day is so overwhelming that enduring it is questioned, implying that reverential dread is the only rational inner response to God's terrifying, irresistible power.
God commands the land not to fear, replacing inner dread with gladness because he has acted on their behalf; the prohibition of fear is grounded in God's great deeds, not in the situation itself.

Even the beasts of the field are told not to fear, as renewed fertility signals God's restorative provision; fear is shown to be an inner state that responds to threat and dissolves when abundance returns.

The cosmic signs preceding the great and awesome day of the Lord are described with the same root as fear/awe, depicting the coming day as one that will fill all observers with overwhelming reverential terror.
A trumpet blast in a city naturally produces inner trembling in all who hear it; the rhetorical question implies that divine disaster should produce the same instinctive inner alarm in Israel.

Just as a lion's roar instinctively compels fear in any hearer, God's spoken word compels prophetic proclamation; reverential fear is presented as an unavoidable inner reflex when divine power is displayed.

The storm drives the mariners to fear, prompting each one to cry out to his own god and to jettison cargo—fear here is an inner crisis that immediately redirects attention and action toward the divine.

Jonah identifies himself as one who fears the Lord, the Creator of sea and land; this reverent fear defines his identity and relationship to God even while he is actively fleeing from divine commission.

The sailors' fear becomes exceedingly great when they learn Jonah is fleeing the Lord who made the sea they sail; knowledge of God's identity and Jonah's flight intensifies the inner terror already stirred by the storm.

The sailors' exceedingly great fear is named as the inner emotional state produced by discovering they are caught up in a man's flight from the Lord of creation—fear here is the felt weight of divine displeasure.

After the storm stills, the sailors' fear of the Lord intensifies greatly, transforming from storm-terror into reverential worship expressed in sacrifice and vows—fear becomes the inner ground of devotion.
The great fear the men experienced is linked directly to their offering sacrifice and making vows; the intensity of reverential awe is what moves them from observers to worshipers of Israel's God.

Jonah describes God's breakers and waves crashing over him in the deep as the experience of being overwhelmed; the waves are instruments of divine pressure bearing down on the inner person in extremity.

In the coming age of peace, no one will make anyone afraid; trembling fear is here defined by its absence—the ideal future is one where the inner person is wholly free from the threat-driven dread that now afflicts them.
The nations crawl out of their strongholds in fearful retreat, shrinking like serpents—this cringing, creeping movement embodies the inner collapse of pride into abject terror before the Lord.
The nations ultimately stand in awe of God, their prior defiance dissolved into reverential fear; the inner disposition shifts from arrogance to trembling submission before divine power.
The nations come trembling out of their strongholds and turn in dread to the Lord; this dread is the inner posture of forced recognition of God's supremacy after their defeat and humiliation.

The nations come trembling from their strongholds toward the Lord; the trembling is a whole-body inner collapse of confidence that accompanies their compelled recognition of God's sovereign authority.

God commands the prophet to look and be utterly astounded at what he is doing among the nations; the astonishment is an inner cognitive and emotional overwhelm at a divine work that defies all expectation.

The Babylonians are described as inherently terrible and fearsome, projecting an inner quality of terrifying power outward; they are dread-inspiring in themselves, a force that generates fear in all they encounter.

The Babylonians are called fearsome because they derive justice and dignity from themselves alone; their self-sovereignty makes them objects of dread, as they answer to no higher authority that could restrain them.

The violence Lebanon suffered will recoil upon its perpetrator and overwhelm him; the verse implies that those who imposed terror will themselves be struck with dismaying destruction—inner dread as just recompense.

Habakkuk confesses that hearing the report of God's deeds fills him with reverential fear; this fear is an inner response to God's powerful works in history, not a paralyzing dread but an awe that prompts intercession.

The tents of Cushan tremble at God's theophanic approach; this visible shaking of nations' encampments externalizes the inner trembling that divine manifestation produces in those who witness his power.

The prophet's body trembles, his lips quiver, and his bones decay at the sound of God's approaching judgment; trembling saturates the entire inner and physical person as fear registers its full physiological impact.

On the day of the Lord's wrath, wealth cannot deliver anyone; the verse implies sudden dismay as all earthly security proves worthless—the inner being is left with nothing to ward off the terror of God's consuming fire.

The Lord will be awesome against the nations, stripping their gods of power; this awesome terror of God is the inner-collapse-inducing force that brings all peoples to bow before him in their own places.

God expected his correction to produce fear and obedience in Jerusalem's heart, but instead they became more corrupt; the absence of fear is identified as the inner failure that blocked repentance and acceptance of discipline.

The restored remnant of Israel will graze and rest with no one to make them afraid; freedom from trembling fear is the inner hallmark of their redeemed condition, paired with integrity in speech and deed.

With the Lord present in Jerusalem's midst and enemies cleared away, the people are told they shall never again fear evil; divine presence removes the inner ground of dread, replacing it with secure peace.

On the day of restoration Jerusalem is commanded not to fear and to keep its hands from going limp; fear here is the inner debilitating weakness that drains will and strength, countered by God's saving presence.

After hearing the prophetic word, the remnant obeyed and the people feared the Lord; reverential fear is shown as the inner response that aligns the will with obedience to God's voice delivered through his messenger.

With God's Spirit remaining in their midst, the people are commanded not to fear; the promise of divine presence is the stated reason fear should not take hold, grounding courage in covenantal relationship.

The four craftsmen come to terrify the horns that had scattered Judah; the intended inner effect on the enemy nations is terror that breaks their lifted power and prevents further oppression of God's people.

God's promise to save and bless Judah and Israel is the reason fear should not grip their inner being; 'fear not' is grounded in divine reversal of their cursed status, calling them to strength rather than anxious dread.

God's renewed purpose to bring good to Jerusalem is given as the direct basis for the command not to fear; the inner state of fear is addressed by pointing the will toward God's deliberate, benevolent intention.

Ashkelon sees divine judgment fall on neighboring cities and is struck with fear; the watching city's inner being recoils in terror as its hopes are confounded and the threat draws near to its own borders.

God strikes enemy horses with panic, a divinely induced bewilderment that disables their riders' ability to act coherently; bewilderment here is a weapon—an inner cognitive chaos God unleashes to protect Judah.

God asks the priests where the fear due to him as master is, indicating that reverential fear is an expected inner disposition of those under authority; its absence in the priests signals a broken relational posture toward God.
Fear here is the reverent deference owed to a master by a servant; God asks priests where this inner disposition toward him is, exposing its absence in their contemptuous worship.
The priests treat worship as a burdensome hardship, expressing inner weariness and contempt rather than devotion, and bring defective offerings that reveal their disdainful attitude toward God.
God's name will evoke genuine reverent fear among all nations, implicitly contrasting with Israel's priests who fail to render that same awe, as his greatness as King demands it universally.
The covenant with Levi was grounded in fear before God—a disposition of awe so complete that the priest stood in reverence of God's name, leaving no room for dismay or withdrawal from that stance.
Levi's reverential fear was the covenant response to God's gift of life and peace; his inner being was oriented in awe toward God, expressed in standing in reverence of his name.

Levi feared God as the living covenantal response to receiving life and peace; this reverence was not merely outward but an inner orientation of awe sustaining his priestly standing before God's name.

Fear is named as the very substance of the covenant God gave Levi—it was a covenant defined by and sustained through reverential awe, paired with the practical response of standing in awe of God's name.
Fear forms the defining character of Levi's covenant with God—it was a relationship structured around reverential awe, with Levi's inner being wholly oriented in trembling respect before God's name.

Those who oppress the vulnerable and swear falsely are characterized precisely by the absence of fear of God; their injustice flows from an inner disposition that does not reckon with divine judgment.

Those who fear the Lord are identified as a community whose inner reverence expresses itself in mutual encouragement and esteem for God's name, so notable that God records them in a book of remembrance.
Fear of God's name is the inner orientation that positions a person to receive his healing and redemption; it is the condition of the heart from which joyful deliverance flows.

The coming day of the Lord is described as great and awesome—evoking dread of divine intervention—with Elijah sent beforehand, implying the need for inner preparation before that terrifying day arrives.
Joseph's inner fear about taking Mary as wife is addressed by the angel's command not to fear; the fear is an anxious hesitation in the will, dispelled by divine revelation and reassurance.
Joseph's fear is a protective, prudential dread that restrained his will from going to Judea under Archelaus's dangerous rule, operating as an inner check on his decision-making.
Jesus names the disciples' panic in the storm as timidity, linking it directly to little faith—their inner cowardice in the face of danger reveals a failure of trust in him who was present with them.
The crowds' fear is an awe-struck response to witnessing divine authority at work in healing; it moves inward immediately into glorifying God, so fear here functions as the gateway to worship.

Jesus commands disciples not to let fear of persecutors grip their inner being, grounding that command in the certainty that all hidden things will be revealed—truth's inevitability displaces fear of men.

Fear of those who can only kill the body is contrasted with fear of God who can destroy soul and body in hell; the verse locates the soul as the deeper seat of the person, making God the only worthy object of ultimate fear.

The command not to fear is grounded in the disciples' immense worth before God; fear of men or circumstances is to be displaced by awareness of God's personal care, redirecting the inner disposition away from dread.
Tribulation and persecution expose the rootlessness of a shallow inner response to the word; external pressure reveals that no deep work has taken hold in the person, causing immediate apostasy.
Herod's fear of the crowd restrained his will from executing John; the inner fear of popular opinion acted as a controlling force over his decision, overriding his murderous desire.

The disciples' fear upon seeing Jesus walk on water is a terrified cry of alarm—their inner perception misidentifies him as a ghost, and fear is the overwhelming emotional response expressed outwardly in a shout.
Jesus immediately addresses the disciples' fear with his self-disclosure 'it is I,' indicating that the fear arose from not recognizing him; the antidote to the inner alarm is identity and presence.
Peter's fear arises when he sees the wind rather than keeping his eyes on Jesus; the inner shift of attention from Christ to the threatening circumstances triggers fear that leads to sinking.

The disciples' terror at the transfiguration is so overwhelming that they fall prostrate; the divine voice produces a physical collapse, marking the inner being's total inability to stand before manifest glory.
Jesus' touch and command 'have no fear' come immediately after the disciples' prostrate terror; his physical contact and word directly address and dispel the fear gripping their inner persons.
The religious leaders' fear of the crowd's reaction controls their public answer about John's baptism; social dread of the people's opinion paralyzes their will and shapes their speech.

Fear of the crowds as prophetic authorities restrains the leaders from arresting Jesus; the inner dread of public reaction overrides their hostile intent, demonstrating how social fear governs their agency.
Tribulation here is externally imposed suffering—being delivered to death and universal hatred—that presses in on disciples from outside, testing their endurance for bearing Christ's name.

The great tribulation is described as unparalleled suffering in all of history; its intensity surpasses anything before or after, representing extreme external pressure that will bear down on those alive at that time.

The tribulation of those days is portrayed as a period of suffering so severe that cosmic signs immediately follow its end; it functions as the crushing pressure that precedes the final disruption of all created order.

The servant's fear drove him to bury the talent rather than risk using it; his inner dread of the master's harshness paralyzed his will and produced passive self-preservation instead of faithful stewardship.

Jesus begins to be sorrowful and troubled in Gethsemane; the distress is a deeply agitated inner state engaging his soul as he faces the approaching suffering, manifesting as anguished heaviness of spirit.

The centurion and guards are filled with awe at the earthquake and accompanying signs at Jesus' death; their fear is a reverent, confession-producing response that leads to the declaration 'Truly this was the Son of God.'

The guards' fear of the angel is so overwhelming that their bodies fail—they tremble and become like dead men; the fear produces a total physical and volitional collapse in the presence of divine power.

The angel tells the women not to be afraid, acknowledging their fear at the empty tomb and angelic presence, then immediately redirects their inner attention to the resurrection news as the proper response.

The women depart from the tomb holding fear and great joy simultaneously; fear arises from the overwhelming encounter with resurrection reality, yet coexists with joy, showing both emotions active in the inner person at once.

Jesus commands the women 'do not be afraid' as he commissions them to tell his brothers; he directly addresses their lingering fear, replacing it with purposeful mission as the appropriate inner response to resurrection.

Tribulation and persecution arising from the word expose the rootlessness of a superficial hearer; external pressure acts as a test of inner depth, and those without root fall away immediately under it.

Jesus equates the disciples' fear in the storm with having no faith; their inner cowardice and trust-failure are presented as the same deficiency, with timidity standing in direct contrast to confident reliance on him.

The disciples are filled with great fear after Jesus calms the storm; the fear is not dread of danger but overwhelming awe at supernatural authority over creation, prompting wonder about who Jesus is.

Great fear fills the disciples as they witness Jesus commanding wind and sea into calm; the fear is an awe-filled inner response to displayed divine power that immediately issues in questioning his identity.

The crowd's fear upon seeing the formerly demon-possessed man restored to his right mind is an awe-mixed dread; the transformation of the man makes them afraid of the supernatural power present in Jesus.

The healed woman approaches Jesus in fear and trembling—her inner state combines reverential dread and physical shaking—yet she speaks the whole truth, showing fear here yielding to courageous disclosure before him.

Jesus tells Jairus not to fear but only to believe; fear and faith are set as inner opposites, with fear threatening to displace trust at the point of the greatest threat—his daughter's reported death.
Herod is greatly perplexed by John's message yet keeps listening gladly; his inner state is a confused, unresolved tension between attraction to John's teaching and inability to act on it.
Herod's fear of John is grounded in his recognition of John's righteousness and holiness; the fear operates protectively in his will, causing him to keep John safe rather than harm him.

All the disciples are terrified at seeing Jesus walk on water; he immediately speaks to address that inner terror, replacing it with the reassurance of his identity and presence—'it is I, do not be afraid.'
Peter's inability to know what to say at the transfiguration is explained by the terror gripping the disciples; the fear so overwhelms their inner faculties that thought and speech are disabled.

The crowd's great amazement at seeing Jesus produces an immediate physical response of running toward him; the awe-struck wonder engages both perception and body, expressing astonishment at his unexpected presence.
Fear operates as an internal barrier in the disciples' will, preventing them from asking Jesus to clarify what they did not understand — their inner apprehension silences the question.
Fear grips those following Jesus on the road to Jerusalem, producing an inner dread at what lies ahead, set against the amazement of those closer to him.
The chief priests and scribes fear Jesus because of the crowd's astonishment at his teaching — their fear is a self-preserving dread that drives them toward plotting his destruction.
Fear of the people restrains the leaders from answering honestly about John's baptism, showing that social dread overrides their willingness to speak their true mind.
Fear of the crowd checks the leaders' intent to arrest Jesus, making dread of public reaction the force that overrides their hostile will and causes them to withdraw.
