Tribulation here is an unparalleled external crushing pressure on humanity — unprecedented in scale from creation until now — that bears down on those living through the end-time crisis.
This tribulation marks a cosmic turning point: after it passes, the very heavens lose their light, indicating the pressure it exerts on creation is the threshold event of the age's end.
Jesus begins to be deeply distressed in Gethsemane — this is a severe inner anguish, initiated in the soul as he faces what is coming, paired with being troubled at the same moment.
In Gethsemane Jesus is struck with an overwhelming, awe-laden horror — 'greatly distressed' captures a profound inner shuddering as the weight of his coming suffering presses on his spirit.
Entering the tomb and seeing a young man in dazzling white, the women are seized with alarmed awe — the unexpected supernatural sight strikes their inner being with startled, reverential shock.
The angel's command 'do not be alarmed' addresses the women's awe-struck inner state directly, urging them to move from shocked terror to purposeful belief in the resurrection reality.

Trembling seizes the women together with astonishment as they flee the tomb — it is an involuntary bodily and inner quaking produced by the overwhelming reality of the empty tomb and the angelic encounter.
Fear drives the women to silence and flight from the tomb — it is a paralysing inner state that overpowers their will, leaving them unable to speak to anyone about what they witnessed.
Fear falls upon Zechariah the moment he sees the angel — it is an involuntary inner experience that descends on him, overwhelming him at the sudden appearance of the divine messenger.
The angel commands Zechariah not to fear, directly addressing the inner alarm that the angelic appearance triggered, and redirecting his inner state by announcing that his prayer is answered.
The angel's 'do not be afraid' to Mary acknowledges that the divine encounter has produced fear in her inner being, and counters it with the reassurance of God's favour resting on her.
Fear of God here is the enduring inner orientation that receives divine mercy across generations — it is a reverent posture of the soul directed toward God rather than a momentary dread.

Fear comes upon all the neighbours in response to the supernatural events surrounding John's birth — a communal inner awe spreading through the whole region, prompting widespread talk of what had happened.
The shepherds are filled with great fear when the angel appears and the divine glory shines around them — the inner being is overwhelmed and filled to capacity by terror at the supernatural presence.

The angel's command 'fear not' addresses the terror that has seized the shepherds and replaces it with joyful news — fear is named as the inner state to be displaced by the good news of Christ's birth.
Jesus commands Simon not to be afraid after the miraculous catch of fish, addressing the inner alarm that the miracle has produced and redirecting his attention toward a new calling.
Awe fills all those who witness the healing of the paralysed man — fear here is the inner dimension of wonder and reverence that accompanies witnessing a divine act, paired with praise to God.
Fear seizes everyone who witnesses the raising of the widow's son — it is a sudden, encompassing inner grip of reverential dread before God's power, immediately expressed as glorifying God.
After Jesus stills the storm, the disciples are afraid and marvel — their fear is an inner response to his supernatural authority over creation, raising the urgent question of who he truly is.

Seeing the formerly demon-possessed man restored and in his right mind, the people are afraid — the fear is an inner recoil before a display of divine power that exceeds their categories of understanding.
Great fear seizes the Gerasenes after the demons are cast out and the swine destroyed — it is an overwhelming inner dread at the display of supernatural power that drives them to ask Jesus to leave.
Jesus opposes fear to faith — 'do not fear; only believe' — naming fear as the inner enemy of trust that threatens to overcome Jairus as he hears his daughter has died.

Herod is perplexed in his mind because conflicting reports about Jesus — including that John has risen — leave him unable to resolve what is happening, producing an inner state of bewildered uncertainty.

The disciples are afraid as they enter the cloud at the Transfiguration — the overwhelming divine presence directly produces fear as an involuntary inner response to being enveloped by the supernatural.
The disciples are afraid to ask Jesus about the saying they cannot understand — fear silences their inquiry, functioning as an inner barrier between their confusion and the clarification they need.

Jesus tells his friends not to fear those who can only kill the body — he limits the proper object of fear, redirecting inner dread away from human power that has no ultimate authority over the person.

Jesus commands fear directed at God alone, who has authority to cast into hell after death — this fear is the right orientation of the inner being, calibrated to ultimate power rather than mortal threat.
Jesus commands 'fear not' on the basis of the disciples' value to God — the inner anxiety is addressed by reminding them that divine care extends to the smallest detail of their lives.

Jesus addresses the little flock's fear with the assurance of the Father's good pleasure — the command 'fear not' speaks to an inner anxiety about the kingdom, countered by God's sovereign generosity.
The unjust judge neither fears God nor respects man — the absence of this fear defines his inner character as one who acknowledges no higher authority and operates with no reverential constraint.
The judge admits to himself that he neither fears God nor respects man — this self-acknowledged inner state of no reverential fear is offered as his reason for his behaviour, yet he acts anyway.
The servant confesses that fear of his severe master drove him to bury the mina rather than invest it — inner dread of punishment paralysed his will and produced inaction rather than faithful risk.

Fear of the people restrains the scribes and chief priests from arresting Jesus on the spot — their hostile inner intent is checked by dread of public reaction, which functions as an external social constraint.
Jesus commands his disciples not to be terrified by wars and tumults — the inner being is not to be shaken into panic by alarming events, because such things must happen before the end comes.

Fear is so overwhelming at the sight of cosmic signs that people faint — it is an inner experience of dread so intense it overcomes the body, produced by foreboding of catastrophic events descending on the world.
Fear of the people prevents the chief priests and scribes from acting on their murderous intent against Jesus — dread of popular reaction restrains their will and forces them to seek a covert method.

The penitent criminal rebukes the other by appealing to fear of God — in the face of shared condemnation and death, reverent fear of God is the inner posture that should govern how one speaks and acts.
The women are perplexed at the empty tomb — finding no body where they expected one leaves their minds in a state of confused disorientation, unable to account for what they are experiencing.
The women are frightened and bow to the ground at the angelic appearance — their inner fear expresses itself in physical prostration, showing that the terror has overwhelmed both spirit and body.
The disciples are startled and frightened, thinking they see a spirit — fear here is an inner alarm triggered by the impossible appearance of the risen Jesus, producing a supernatural dread.
Being startled precedes and feeds into being frightened — the initial inner shock at Jesus's appearance triggers a deeper terror rooted in the belief that they are seeing a ghost.
The disciples are frightened when they see Jesus walking on the sea — the impossible sight strikes their inner being with sudden fear, the natural response to what seems to violate the known order.
Jesus commands 'do not be afraid' after the disciples see him walking on water — his self-identification directly addresses and is meant to dissolve the inner fear his appearance has produced.
Fear of the Jews suppresses open speech about Jesus — it is an inner social dread that controls what people will say publicly, silencing their true mind to avoid the threat of Jewish authority.

The parents fear the Jews because confessing Jesus means expulsion from the synagogue — their inner dread of social and religious exclusion governs their speech and makes them deflect questioning to their son.
The command 'fear not' addresses the inner disposition of dread or alarm, calling Zion's daughter to release that fearful inner state because the arriving king brings peace, not threat.
The disciples' mutual glancing expresses an inner cognitive bewilderment—they cannot identify who among them Jesus means, leaving their minds suspended in unresolved uncertainty.
Jesus contrasts his given peace with an inner timidity or cowardly shrinking, commanding the heart not to yield to that fearful recoiling, positioning the will against such inner collapse.

The birthing woman's anguish is so completely displaced by joy at her child's arrival that she no longer holds it in memory—inner pressure is real but temporary, overtaken by a stronger positive state.
Tribulation is declared an inevitable inner pressure belonging to life in the world, yet Christ's overcoming provides the basis for courage—peace in him directly counters that pressing weight.
Hearing the claim that Jesus is the Son of God intensifies Pilate's already existing fear, showing that new information amplifies an inner dread already present in his conscience.
Joseph's discipleship was hidden because fear of the Jews governed his public behavior, showing fear operating as an inner constraint that suppressed outward allegiance and shaped his choices.
The disciples' fear of the Jews caused them to lock their doors, revealing fear functioning as an inner force that drives protective, isolating behavior and shapes their physical environment.
The Pentecost crowd's perplexity arises alongside amazement as they witness something entirely outside their interpretive framework, leaving the mind unable to assign meaning to what it observes.
Awe came upon every soul as wonders and signs multiplied through the apostles, indicating fear-as-reverence permeating the inner person universally in response to divine power at work.

Recognizing the healed man as the familiar beggar filled the crowd with wonder and amazement, showing that the inner state is triggered by the stark contrast between what they knew and what they now see.
The people were utterly astounded—their inner state so overwhelming that it propelled them physically to run toward Peter and John, showing astonishment as a force that drives outward movement.
Ananias's sudden death produced great fear that came upon all who heard, showing that fear spread as an internal response through a community when divine judgment became concretely visible.
Great fear fell upon the whole church and all who heard, indicating that the twin deaths of Ananias and Sapphira generated a widespread inner sobriety and dread before God's holiness.
The captain and chief priests were greatly perplexed when told the prison was empty, their minds unable to account for what had happened and left wondering what the outcome would be.
The captain refrained from using force because he feared the people might stone him, showing fear of physical harm as an inner constraint that moderated the exercise of official authority.
The verse notes that God rescued Joseph from all his afflictions, framing the pressure he endured as real and multiple, but overcome by divine intervention that reversed his circumstances entirely.

Moses trembled and did not dare look when confronted with God's self-identification, showing that divine holiness produced an involuntary bodily trembling paired with an inner paralysis of will before God.
The Jerusalem disciples feared Saul because they did not believe his conversion, revealing fear rooted in prior experience and distrust that prevented them from accepting his claim of discipleship.

Walking in the fear of the Lord is paired with comfort of the Spirit and results in the church multiplying, showing this inner reverence as an active, life-ordering orientation that sustains community growth.

Cornelius feared God with all his household, and this inner reverence expressed itself outwardly in generous almsgiving and continual prayer, showing fear of God as the motivating core of a devout life.

Cornelius stared at the angel in terror, an inner shock so intense that his first words are a question of submission, showing fear of the divine as a destabilizing confrontation that suspends normal composure.
Peter was inwardly perplexed about the vision's meaning, locating the confusion explicitly inside himself—his mind working on the problem without resolution even as the answer arrives at the gate.
Cornelius is described as a God-fearing man whose character is publicly recognized, indicating that inner reverence toward God was a defining and observable trait of his whole way of living.

Anyone who fears God and does what is right is acceptable to him regardless of nationality, showing that inner reverence is the foundational inner posture from which right action flows and by which God accepts a person.

The persecution that scattered believers after Stephen's death is the pressure referenced, functioning as an external force that displaced believers but ironically extended the word's reach geographically.
Disciples are told they must enter the kingdom through many tribulations, framing inner and external pressure as the unavoidable path of discipleship rather than an exception or sign of failure.
The jailer rushed in trembling with fear and prostrated himself before Paul and Silas, his body expressing the overwhelming inner terror produced by the earthquake and its implications for his life.

Fear fell on all residents of Ephesus when they heard of the defeat of the Jewish exorcists, and this inner awe led to the exaltation of Christ's name, showing fear as a gateway to reverence and worship.

The Spirit repeatedly testified to Paul that afflictions await him in every city, framing the coming pressure as divinely foreknown and personally communicated, not unexpected—yet still real and painful.

Felix became alarmed as Paul reasoned about righteousness, self-control, and coming judgment, showing that moral and eschatological argument penetrated his inner being and produced a frightened, avoidant response.

Festus was at a loss how to investigate Paul's case, his perplexity being practical and judicial—he lacked the conceptual framework to adjudicate religious disputes, leaving his reasoning at an impasse.

Tribulation is declared the consequence for those who do evil, presented as a coming outer-and-inner pressure that functions as divine retributive judgment applied universally regardless of ethnic identity.
Distress is paired with tribulation as a second dimension of divine judgment on evildoers, suggesting a narrowing, constrictive anguish that accompanies the broader pressure of God's wrath.

The absence of fear of God is located 'before their eyes'—in their inner orientation and perception—indicating that godless humanity lacks the reverential awareness that should govern all thought and conduct.

Believers are said to rejoice in sufferings because that inner and outer pressure produces endurance, reframing tribulation not as pointless pain but as a generative inner process leading to character.

The Spirit of adoption displaces the spirit of slavery and its associated fear, showing that adoption-sonship removes fear as an inner relational posture and replaces it with the intimacy of crying 'Abba.'

Tribulation is listed as one of multiple forces that might seem to separate believers from Christ's love, but the verse implies it cannot—the pressure is real but powerless to sever that fundamental bond.

Distress appears alongside tribulation as a potential separator from Christ's love, naming the constrictive, pressing anguish of hardship as something tested against but unable to overcome divine love.

Fear is commanded as the antidote to pride for Gentile believers standing by faith, positioning it as a healthy, stabilizing inner reverence that keeps a person from arrogant self-reliance before God.
Believers are commanded to be patient in tribulation, treating the inner and outer pressure not as something to escape but to endure steadily, held together by persistent prayer and hope.
The fear of governing authority is presented as a motivating inner force tied to conduct—those who do good need not fear rulers, making the presence or absence of this fear a gauge of one's behavior.

Rulers are described as a terror to bad conduct, not good—showing that the fear they inspire is directional, targeting wrongdoing and functioning as a socially ordering inner deterrent for the community.

The wrongdoer is told to be afraid of the governing authority who bears the sword as God's avenger, showing fear here as a rational inner response to real penal consequence wielded by legitimate power.

Respect is named as something owed to its proper recipient, placing fear-as-honor within a framework of relational and civic obligations rather than raw dread, locating it in ordered social conscience.

Paul describes his ministry arrival as marked by weakness, fear, and much trembling, indicating that his inner state combined apprehension with bodily manifestation, framing his ministry in conscious vulnerability.

Paul names fear as one element of his inner state when he came to Corinth, paired with trembling and weakness, showing that his proclamation arose not from inner boldness but from a place of genuine inner fragility.
Married people will experience worldly troubles, Paul says, treating the pressure of married life as a predictable inner and relational burden that he would spare his readers if he could.

God comforts Paul in all his affliction so that the comfort received becomes the resource for comforting others in affliction—the inner pressure is real, but divine comfort transforms the sufferer into a comforter.

The affliction in Asia was so crushing that it burdened Paul beyond his strength to the point of despairing of life itself, showing pressure reaching its extreme inner limit where survival itself seemed impossible.
The pressure Paul felt was seated deep in the heart, expressed through anguish and many tears, yet it was driven not by hostility but by an overflow of love for the Corinthians.
Perplexity strikes the mind with no clear way forward, yet the verse shows it stops short of full despair—inner confusion is real but not total, leaving the will uncrushed.
The pressure of present affliction is reframed as light and momentary against an eternal glory it is actively producing, giving the suffering a purposive, transformative weight rather than mere pain.
Knowing the fear of the Lord shapes the will toward persuasion of others; it is a motivating inner awareness of divine accountability that drives outward conduct and can be known by conscience.
Afflictions are listed as defining pressures borne by God's servants alongside hardships and calamities, forming the visible credential of authentic ministry through endurance.
Hardship appears as one of several crushing constraints God's servants endure, commending themselves not by comfort but by steadfast bearing of straitened, difficult conditions.
Fear of God is the motivating inner disposition that drives self-purification of body and spirit, orienting the whole person toward completed holiness as its goal.
Even within the crush of affliction, Paul's inner being overflows with joy, showing that external pressure does not extinguish but coexists with deep emotional fullness.
Fear is located 'within' as an inward experience alongside external conflicts, describing an internal climate of dread or anxiety that the body itself endured with no rest.
Fear appears as one of several intense inner responses produced by godly grief—a heightened, conscience-stirred alertness arising from genuine sorrow over wrongdoing.
Trembling describes the bodily-emotional state with which the Corinthians received Titus, paired with fear and expressing the reverent, awe-struck reception given to a servant of God.
Fear accompanied by trembling characterizes the inner stance with which the Corinthians received Titus, signaling a reverent, awed obedience rather than casual or reluctant compliance.
Severe affliction forms the crushing external and inner pressure that tests the Macedonians, yet paradoxically their joy and generosity overflow from within it, not despite it.
Pressure here is the burden laid on one party while another is eased; the inner-being content is the sense of being unfairly weighed down, which fairness is meant to correct.
Paul's fear is a cognitive-emotional apprehension directed at the Corinthians' vulnerability to deception, engaging his mind and will in protective concern for their devotion to Christ.
Hardships are embraced willingly for Christ's sake alongside other constraints, and the inner posture is contentment, inverting the normal experience of distress into an occasion of strength.
Paul's fear is an anticipatory dread rooted in thought about what he may find—relational disorder and sin—engaging the mind with anxious expectation before the visit even happens.
Peter's fearing moved his will to withdrawal and separation; fear of the circumcision party's opinion overrode principle, showing how social dread can govern behavior against conviction.
Paul's fear is an inner apprehension about the outcome of his ministry effort—a worried uncertainty about whether his labor among the Galatians has been rendered futile.
Perplexity is an inward state of uncertainty about persons—Paul is at a loss about the Galatians' condition—engaging his mind and emotion with genuine bewilderment that changes his intended tone.
Paul's suffering exerts an inward pressure that could cause the Ephesians to 'lose heart'; he reframes it as their glory, turning potential discouragement into a reason for inner steadfastness.
Reverence for Christ is the inner motivating fear that grounds mutual submission among believers, directing the will outward to others from an inward awe of Christ.
The wife's respect for her husband is the commanded inner-outer disposition corresponding to the husband's love, pointing to a deep relational reverence as the fitting stance of her will and heart.
Trembling accompanies fear as the bodily-emotional expression of a sincere, reverent heart directed toward earthly masters as if serving Christ, linking inner awe to outward obedience.
Fear paired with trembling and a sincere heart describes the inner disposition of reverence that should govern a bondservant's obedience, ultimately oriented toward Christ himself.

Pressure is inflicted intentionally on Paul by rivals who think his imprisonment adds to his distress; the affliction is an inner and circumstantial burden others seek to increase through selfish motives.
The believers are urged not to be frightened by opponents—the inner movement of alarm is named as something to be actively resisted, and its absence signals salvation and divine backing.
Trembling accompanies fear as the inner-bodily tone of serious, awed engagement with one's own salvation, indicating that working out salvation demands earnest gravity rather than casual ease.
Fear, paired with trembling, describes the reverent, serious inner posture with which believers are to pursue their salvation—an awe-filled engagement of will, not terror that paralyzes.
Epaphroditus was distressed because the Philippians had heard of his illness; the distress is relational—an inner anguish arising from concern about how his condition has troubled those he loves.

Paul's trouble is the burden of pressure he bore, and the Philippians' sharing in it is described as an act of kindness, indicating that the affliction was a real inner and circumstantial weight others could enter.

Paul's afflictions are borne in his flesh on behalf of the church, filling up something still lacking in Christ's sufferings; the pressure is bodily and purposive, given meaning through solidarity with Christ's mission.

Fearing the Lord is the sincere inner orientation that replaces performative people-pleasing; it relocates the will's true master from human observers to God, producing genuine rather than surface obedience.

The Thessalonians received the word under much affliction, yet their inner state was simultaneously joy from the Holy Spirit—showing pressure and Spirit-given delight coexisting in the same moment of reception.

Believers are warned not to be shaken or moved internally by afflictions, implying these pressures carry the power to destabilize faith; but Paul frames them as a destined, expected part of Christian life.

Affliction and distress pressed upon Paul's inner life, but the Thessalonians' faith brought comfort, showing the inner burden of pressure can be relieved by relational and spiritual news from others.
The Thessalonians are enduring afflictions with steadfastness and faith; the pressure is ongoing and real, yet their inner endurance under it is the very thing being boasted about in the churches.
God will repay affliction to those who afflict believers; here pressure becomes the instrument of divine justice, showing it has moral weight and will be turned back on the ones who inflicted it.

Public rebuke of persistent sin produces fear in onlookers; this fear is a deterrent response in the conscience of others, making the community's inner moral vigilance sharper through visible accountability.

Timidity is contrasted with the spirit God gives—power, love, and self-control; it is identified as a spirit-level disposition that God has not implanted, making it a failure of inner constitution rather than circumstance.
Fear of death held people in lifelong inner slavery; it is a pervasive, dominating dread that enslaves the whole person across a lifetime until Christ's deliverance breaks its grip.

Believers are called to an inner alertness—a godly fearing—lest they fall short of God's rest, engaging the will and conscience in watchful, serious self-examination before an open but fragile promise.
Fearful expectation of judgment is the inner experience awaiting those who sin willfully after receiving truth—an anticipatory dread lodged in the mind directed toward coming fiery condemnation.
Falling into the hands of the living God is declared a fearful thing, pointing to an overwhelming, justified inner dread produced by direct encounter with divine judgment and absolute power.

Affliction is publicly imposed on believers through exposure to reproach, creating both personal inner pressure and a shared burden with others who suffer similarly—a communal bearing of shame and hardship.
Moses' parents were not afraid of the king's edict; their faith overrode the inner impulse of fear that royal authority would normally generate, freeing their will to act protectively.
Moses left Egypt not fearing the king's anger; his inner vision of the invisible God displaced the dread a powerful earthly ruler would normally command, making faith the operative inner faculty.
Moses was terrified by the sight at Sinai—the terror was so overwhelming it moved him to confess trembling with fear, showing the theophany produced extreme, whole-person dread in even the mediator.
Moses trembled with fear at the terrifying sight of Sinai, the trembling being a bodily and inner expression of being overwhelmed by the divine presence—fear made physically manifest.

The sight at Sinai was so fearful that Moses confessed trembling with fear; what is fearful here is the overwhelming divine presence itself, which struck even the chosen mediator with uncontrollable dread.

Reverence and awe (deos) are the fitting inner disposition for approaching God in worship, prompted by gratitude for receiving an unshakeable kingdom—fear here is not dread but profound, worshipful respect.
The believer's inner fear of what humans can do is expelled by confidence that God is helper; the will resolves 'I will not fear,' grounding courage in divine support rather than human threat.
Affliction here is the crushing external pressure borne by orphans and widows; genuine religion is measured by willingness to enter and relieve that distress rather than avoid it.
Fear is the governing inner posture for conduct during earthly exile, produced by awareness that the Father judges each person's deeds impartially—it shapes behavior from the inside out.
Fearing God is placed at the moral center of a set of relational duties; it is a distinct inner orientation toward God that differs in kind from the honor shown to other humans.

Fear (fobos) here functions as the reverential inner attitude that shapes a servant's submission to masters—extending even to unjust ones—indicating it is conscience-driven, not merely situational compliance.
The inner reverence (fobos) of wives becomes visible to unbelieving husbands through outward respectful and pure conduct, showing that this fear expresses itself as observable character.

Ptoēsis names the alarming inner fright that circumstances can provoke; Sarah's daughters are called not to yield to such frightening feelings, grounding courage in doing good rather than suppressing anxiety by will alone.
Not fearing what is frightening is the inner freedom that marks Sarah's spiritual daughters; doing good is named as the practical path by which that fearlessness is exercised and demonstrated.

Believers who suffer for righteousness are commanded not to fear their persecutors; the inner stance of fear toward opponents is precisely what they are to resist, being blessed rather than intimidated.

Fear of opponents is explicitly forbidden to those who suffer for righteousness; the inner state of dread toward humans is the opposite of the blessed, untroubled composure suffering saints are called to hold.
Reverence (fobos) toward God is the inner manner—paired with gentleness—in which believers are to give reasoned account of their hope, making it a heart-posture that shapes even apologetic speech.

Whoever still fears has not been perfected in love; fear here is an inner state tied to anticipation of punishment, and perfect love drives it out—showing fear and love occupy the same interior space but cannot coexist fully.
Fear is defined as rooted in punishment-anticipation and as incompatible with mature love; perfect love expels it entirely, locating the conflict between fear and love squarely in the inner person.
Fear accompanies the act of showing mercy to those in spiritual danger; it is the inner caution that prevents rescuers from being contaminated by the very sin they are helping others escape.
John names tribulation as a shared inner-and-outer pressure that he and his readers endure together in Jesus; it is not isolating but a communal participation in suffering for the word of God.

John's collapse as if dead at the sight of the glorified Christ is met with 'Fear not'—the command targets the overwhelming dread that the vision produces, with divine self-identification offered as the ground for inner calm.

Tribulation is the crushing pressure of poverty and slander already being experienced by the church in Smyrna; Christ acknowledges it fully, showing that divine knowledge of the inner and outer suffering provides comfort.

Tribulation is imminent imprisonment and testing that will intensify the pressure already felt; the community is told to endure it faithfully unto death rather than letting dread of it govern their will.

Fearing what is about to come is the inner response the church is commanded to refuse; the anticipated suffering—prison, testing, death—is real, but the will to faithfulness is set against the pull of dread.
Great tribulation is the divinely imposed distress threatened as judgment on those who commit spiritual adultery with Jezebel; it functions as punitive pressure designed to drive repentance.

The great tribulation is an extreme ordeal through which this multitude has passed; having come out of it and washed their robes in the Lamb's blood, their suffering is portrayed as refining rather than destroying.
Great fear falls suddenly on the onlookers when the two witnesses are resurrected; it is an involuntary, overwhelming terror produced by witnessing the direct act of divine power reversing death.

The survivors of the earthquake are terrified (emfobos) by the catastrophe; their terror moves them to give glory to God, showing that extreme dread can function as the catalyst for involuntary acknowledgment of divine authority.

Fearing God's name is the defining inner orientation of the prophets, saints, and servants who receive reward at the final judgment; it is the relational posture that distinguishes them from the destroyers who are condemned.

Fearing God is commanded alongside giving him glory and worship in view of the hour of his judgment; it is the appropriate inner response to divine sovereignty over all creation and is directed toward the Creator.

Fearing the Lord is portrayed as the universal, inevitable response to his holiness and revealed righteous acts; the rhetorical question implies that his nature makes such reverence the only fitting inner disposition.

The kings stand at a distance in fear of Babylon's torment; their fear is a self-protective dread of sharing in her punishment, keeping them emotionally paralyzed in lamentation rather than moving toward her.

The merchants' fear of Babylon's torment drives them to stand far off, weeping; it is a self-interested dread of judgment that governs their physical distance and shapes their grief into helpless mourning.
Fearing God is the defining characteristic of his servants called to praise him; it encompasses both the small and great, showing it is the universal inner posture of those who belong to him.

Cowardice (deilos) heads a list of vices that share the lake of fire; it is an inner disposition of faithless timidity that, in this context, is a moral failure equivalent to idolatry or murder—likely denoting apostasy under pressure.
